item.title: SETTING UP AN MLOPS WORKFLOW WITH DAGSHUB AND MLFLOW
item.author: Hasan Tuğra Aykaç
item.content:encoded: Developing and managing machine learning projects can be a complex process for modern data science teams. MLOps has emerged as a powerful approach to streamline this process and automate the workflow from model development to deployment. In this blog post, we will explore step-by-step how to build an effective MLOps workflow using tools like and . By combining Dagshub’s data and model management capabilities with MLflow’s experiment tracking and deployment features, you can make your projects more efficient and scalable. Get ready to dive into the world of MLOps and discover how to optimize your workflow with these powerful tools.Dagshub is a platform that integrates with GitHub, so we need to create a repository on GitHub. You can optionally add a .gitignore and README. I have created a public repository named , and I will proceed with this repository. Now, we clone the repository to our computer, create a Conda environment, and install the libraries listed in the requirements.txt file. If everything is ready, we can start coding and connect our repository to Dagshub.You need to have a Dagshub account; you can log in using your GitHub account or Google account. After logging in, you will be greeted by the Dagshub dashboard.Click on the “Create +” button in the top right corner.In the opened window, click on the “Connect a repository” menu. After clicked you will see two different options: GitHub and Other. Select the GitHub option here.You will see a section like the one in the image above. Select the repository you created and click the Save button.Finally, we are greeted by a section like this on Dagshub. This is the final part related to DagsHub. Now, let’s tackle the coding. For this, I am creating a file named main.py, and I first need to import the necessary libraries.import osimport warningsimport loggingfrom urllib.parse import urlparseimport numpy as npfrom sklearn.model_selection import train_test_splitfrom sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifierfrom sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, f1_scoreimport mlflowimport dagshub# Dagshub integrationdagshub.init(repo_owner='hasan.asus1999', repo_name='MLOPS-Demo', mlflow=True)logging.basicConfig(level=logging.WARN)logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)The line dagshub.init(repo_owner=’hasan.asus1999', repo_name=’MLOPS-Demo’, mlflow=True) is very important, and this code block is provided by DagsHub.On the Dagshub dashboard, click the “Remote” button, navigate to the “Experiments” menu, and select the marked code line under “Using MLflow tracking.”def eval_classification_metrics(actual, pred): acc = accuracy_score(actual, pred) prec = precision_score(actual, pred, average='weighted') rec = recall_score(actual, pred, average='weighted') f1 = f1_score(actual, pred, average='weighted') return acc, prec, rec, f1By writing the eval_classification_metrics function, we will use the classification metrics and make comparisons in MLflow.if __name__ == "__main__": warnings.filterwarnings("ignore") csv_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/uiuc-cse/data-fa14/gh-pages/data/iris.csv" data = pd.read_csv(csv_url) logger.exception("Unable to download CSV. Error: %s", e) # separate dependent and independent variables X = data.drop("species", axis=1) # training/testing distinction X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.25, random_state=42) n_estimators = int(sys.argv[1]) if len(sys.argv) > 1 else 100 max_depth = int(sys.argv[2]) if len(sys.argv) > 2 else None clf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=n_estimators, max_depth=max_depth, random_state=42) clf.fit(X_train, y_train) y_pred = clf.predict(X_test) acc, prec, rec, f1 = eval_classification_metrics(y_test, y_pred) print(f"Random Forest (n_estimators={n_estimators}, max_depth={max_depth}):") print(f" Accuracy: {acc}") print(f" Precision: {prec}") print(f" Recall: {rec}") print(f" F1 Score: {f1}") mlflow.log_param("n_estimators", n_estimators) mlflow.log_param("max_depth", max_depth) mlflow.log_metric("accuracy", acc) mlflow.log_metric("precision", prec) mlflow.log_metric("recall", rec) mlflow.log_metric("f1_score", f1) remote_server_uri = "https://dagshub.com/hasan.asus1999/MLflow-Test.mlflow" mlflow.set_tracking_uri(remote_server_uri) tracking_url_type_store = urlparse(mlflow.get_tracking_uri()).scheme if tracking_url_type_store != "file": mlflow.sklearn.log_model(clf, "model", registered_model_name="RandomForestIrisModel") mlflow.sklearn.log_model(clf, "model")I found it appropriate to use the Iris dataset for this study because it is a fundamental dataset for classification algorithms. This code block, csv_url = “https://raw.githubusercontent.com/uiuc-cse/data-fa14/gh-pages/data/iris.csv" will help me use the Iris dataset, meaning I won’t need to download it from Kaggle or any other source. If you want, you can find more detailed information about the Iris dataset here. Another important point is the Dagshub URI, which is this line of code: remote_server_uri = “https://dagshub.com/hasan.asus1999/MLflow-Test.mlflow". This line is necessary for us to connect to MLflow. So how can we find this in the DagsHub dashboard?On the Dagshub dashboard, click the “Remote” button, navigate to the “Experiments” menu, and select the marked code line under “MLflow Tracking remote”.Now everything is ready. Based on the image above, let’s click on the “Go to mlflow UI” button and go to MLflow.We are greeted by an empty screen; nothing is visible because I haven’t run the relevant Python code. To run my Python code, execute it by typing or .If there are no errors in your DagsHub connections and code, you should see an output like this in the console.Let’s explain the output line by line. Accessing as hasan.asus1999 means you’re authenticated and accessing your correctly. Initialized MLflow to track repo “hasan.asus1999/MLOPS-Demo” means MLflow has been successfully initialized to log all training runs, metrics, and models to your named MLOPS-Demo. Random Forest (n_estimators=100, max_depth=None) indicates the model was trained using the default hyperparameters. If I do not give parameter values ​​to my RandomForest algorithm, my default n_estimators value will be 100 and my max_depth value will be None. We will try it by giving parameters in another example and compare it in MLflow.# Hyperparametersn_estimators = int(sys.argv[1]) if len(sys.argv) > 1 else 100max_depth = int(sys.argv[2]) if len(sys.argv) > 2 else NoneDo you remember the code line from above? That code line allows us to use the default values, but we will break this when we rerun our application shortly. Accuracy: 1.0 Precision: 1.0 F1 Score: 1.0These metrics show perfect scores (1.0), which means the model performed flawlessly on the test data. This is expected for the Iris dataset, which is small, clean, and well-balanced.Successfully registered model ‘RandomForestIrisModel’ is trained model was registered successfully in the MLflow Model Registry under the name RandomForestIrisModel.Created version ‘1’ of model ‘RandomForestIrisModel’. This model is stored as version 1. If you train and log the model again (with different parameters), MLflow will create version 2, 3, etc.Now let’s refresh our MLflow dashboard page and make sure we see something like this.As you can see, everything is fine. In the image above, when we click on the Run Name field next to the orange icon, we can access details such as parameters and metrics.It matches perfectly with the values in our console; this is awesome!Now let’s try different parameters for our RandomForest algorithm. I will run my application this time as follows: This means the n_estimators value will be 150 and the max_depth value will be 5.Now let’s refresh our MLflow dashboard and make sure we can see this image.As you can see we now have two different models. We can visually compare two models by selecting the checkboxes next to the models and clicking the “Compare” button.You can visualize by selecting your parameters and metrics. I tried selecting all parameters and metrics; you can try being more specific.

item.title: Tech stack 101 : Ep-29 : How to optimize your database queries ?
item.author: Nagarjun Gopalaswamy
item.content:encoded: Tech stack 101 : Ep-29 : How to optimize your database queries ?Optimizing a slow-running database query involves identifying and addressing performance bottlenecks in the query execution plan, indexing strategy, database configuration, and data model. Here are some steps you can take to optimize a slow database queryStart by identifying the specific query or queries that are running slow. Use database monitoring tools, query logs, or profiling tools to pinpoint the queries with the longest execution times.This below query retrieves all orders placed after January 1, 2022 and its fetching all the columns.SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_date >= '2022-01-01';Analyze the Execution Plan:Use database query execution analysis tools or built-in database features (e.g., EXPLAIN in SQL databases) to examine the query execution plan. Look for inefficient operations such as full table scans, inefficient joins, or missing indexes.The execution plan shows that a full table scan is being performed on the orders table, which could indicate a potential performance issue for large datasets.EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_date >= '2022-01-01';Once you have identified the inefficient parts of the query execution plan, optimize the query to improve performance. This may involve rewriting the query to use more efficient SQL constructs, adding hints to influence the query optimizer, or restructuring the query to minimize data movement and processing.By adding an ORDER BY clause, the query optimizer may choose to use an index to fulfill the query, resulting in improved performance.SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_date >= '2022-01-01' ORDER BY order_date;Review Indexing Strategy:Evaluate the indexing strategy for the tables involved in the query. Ensure that appropriate indexes are in place to support the query’s filtering, sorting, and joining operations. Consider adding, modifying, or dropping indexes as needed to improve query performance.Adding an index on the order_date column can speed up queries that filter or sort by this column.CREATE INDEX idx_order_date ON orders(order_date);Update table and index statistics to ensure that the query optimizer has accurate information about the data distribution and cardinality. Outdated statistics can lead to suboptimal query plans and performance degradation.Updating table statistics ensures that the query optimizer has accurate information about the data distribution, which can improve query planning.Optimize Database Configuration:Review the database configuration settings such as memory allocation, disk I/O settings, and parallelism settings. Adjust these settings based on the workload characteristics and hardware resources available to the database server.Adjusting the InnoDB buffer pool size can improve the caching of frequently accessed data, leading to better query performance.SET GLOBAL innodb_buffer_pool_size = 2G;Partitioning and Sharding:Consider partitioning or sharding large tables to distribute data across multiple physical or logical partitions. This can improve query performance by reducing the amount of data that needs to be scanned or processed for a given query.Partitioning the orders table by year can improve query performance by limiting the amount of data that needs to be scanned for each query.ALTER TABLE orders PARTITION BY RANGE (YEAR(order_date)) ( PARTITION p1 VALUES LESS THAN (2020), PARTITION p2 VALUES LESS THAN (2021), PARTITION p3 VALUES LESS THAN (2022), PARTITION p4 VALUES LESS THAN MAXVALUE);Optimize Application Code:Review the application code that generates and executes the query. Look for opportunities to optimize the code by reducing the number of database round-trips, minimizing data fetching, and optimizing data processing logic.By fetching only the required columns instead of using SELECT *, the amount of data transferred between the database server and the application can be minimized, improving performance.After implementing optimizations, monitor the query performance to ensure that the changes have the desired effect. Iterate on the optimization process as needed, making further adjustments based on performance metrics and user feedback.Here are some best practices to write SQL queries aimed at good performace : Proper indexing is crucial for efficient query execution. Identify the columns frequently used in WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY clauses, and create indexes on those columns. However, avoid over-indexing, as it can degrade insert and update performance.: Retrieve only the necessary columns and rows by specifying them explicitly in the SELECT statement. Avoid using SELECT * and fetch only the required data to minimize network and disk I/O overhead.: Use JOINs judiciously and ensure that JOIN conditions are optimized. Prefer INNER JOIN over OUTER JOIN when possible, and use appropriate JOIN strategies (e.g., nested loops, hash joins) based on the data distribution and query requirements. Minimize the use of subqueries, especially correlated subqueries, as they can be inefficient. Consider rewriting subqueries as JOINs or using common table expressions (CTEs) to improve query performance. When using subqueries, prefer EXISTS and IN clauses over NOT EXISTS and NOT IN for better performance. EXISTS and IN can be optimized by the database engine more efficiently than NOT EXISTS and NOT IN.Avoid Functions in WHERE Clause: Avoid using functions in the WHERE clause, as they can prevent the use of indexes and lead to full table scans. If possible, precompute or denormalize data to avoid the need for function calls in queries.Minimize Data Manipulation: Minimize data manipulation operations (e.g., sorting, grouping, aggregating) in the database layer. Consider offloading complex calculations and data processing to application code or dedicated data processing tools.Use Parameterized Queries: Use parameterized queries or prepared statements to prevent SQL injection attacks and improve query plan caching. Parameterized queries allow the database engine to reuse query plans, resulting in better performance for repeated executions of the same query with different parameter values.Avoid using cursors and iterative processing in SQL queries whenever possible, as they can be slow and resource-intensive. Instead, use set-based operations and bulk processing techniques to perform data manipulation efficiently.Monitor and Tune Regularly: Monitor query performance using database monitoring tools and query execution plans. Identify and address performance bottlenecks proactively by tuning indexes, optimizing queries, and adjusting database configuration settings as needed.

item.title: Tech stack 101 : Ep-04 AWS database services and its use cases
item.author: Nagarjun Gopalaswamy
item.content:encoded: Tech stack 101 : Ep-04 Types of Databases and different database services offered by AWSA brief introduction to a few types of databases in the industry today.overall the data can be categorized into 3 kinds : 1. Structured data with schema. 2. Semi structured data like documents of xml which is hierarchical3. Unstructured data like logs which are just blobs of data.Depending on the type of data being dealt with, we choose the type of database to be used to store that data. Here are a few different types of database in the market today and its AWS flavors are discussed below.Row Store (Relational Database):In a row store database, data is stored and retrieved row by row. This means that all columns of a single row are stored together.It follows the traditional relational database model where data is organized into tables with rows and columns.Examples of relational databases include MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle Database, SQL Server, etc.Suitable for transactional systems where the primary operation involves querying a small number of rows with many columns.In a column store database, data is stored and retrieved column by column. This means that all values of a single column are stored together.It stores each column of data separately, which can provide significant performance benefits for certain types of queries, especially those that involve aggregations or analytics.Column stores are often used for data warehousing and analytical workloads where queries typically aggregate large amounts of data across many rows but only a few columns.Examples of column store databases include Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery, Apache Cassandra, Vertica, etc.A Graph Database is a type of NoSQL database that uses graph structures for semantic queries with nodes, edges, and properties to represent and store data. Here’s a brief overview along with some common use cases and AWS services related to graph databases: Graph databases organize data into nodes (entities), edges (relationships), and properties (attributes of nodes and edges).: Graph databases typically use graph query languages like Cypher (used in Neo4j) or Gremlin (used in Apache TinkerPop) to perform graph traversals and pattern matching.: Graph databases excel at handling complex relationships and traversing connections between data points efficiently, making them suitable for scenarios with highly interconnected data.Social Networks: Graph databases are well-suited for modeling social network relationships between users, friends, followers, and interests.Recommendation Engines: They can power recommendation systems by analyzing user behavior and preferences, identifying patterns, and suggesting relevant content or products.Fraud Detection: Graph databases can detect fraudulent activities by analyzing patterns and connections between entities such as accounts, transactions, and IP addresses.A Document Database, also known as a Document Store or Document-Oriented Database, is a type of NoSQL database that stores, retrieves, and manages data in the form of semi-structured documents, typically encoded in formats like JSON, BSON, or XML. Here’s an overview of Document Databases and when to use them:Flexible Schema: Document databases offer a flexible schema model, allowing documents within a collection to have varying structures and fields. This flexibility makes it easy to evolve the data model over time without the need for rigid schemas.Document Model: Data is stored in the form of documents, which are self-descriptive, hierarchical structures containing key-value pairs or nested objects. Each document can represent a single entity or record, and related documents can be nested or linked together.Rich Querying: Document databases support rich querying capabilities, allowing developers to perform complex queries, filtering, and indexing based on document contents, nested fields, and array elements.Horizontal Scalability: Many document databases are designed to scale horizontally across distributed clusters of nodes, enabling seamless scaling to accommodate growing data volumes and increasing query loads.High Performance: Document databases often provide fast read and write performance by optimizing data storage and retrieval mechanisms, leveraging indexes, and caching frequently accessed documents.Schema Evolution: With a schema-less or schema-on-read approach, document databases allow developers to insert, update, and query data without predefined schemas, making them suitable for agile development and iterative prototyping.When to Use Document Databases:Semi-Structured Data: Document databases are ideal for managing semi-structured or unstructured data where the schema may vary across different documents or collections, such as user profiles, product catalogs, blog posts, and content management systems.Agile Development: They are well-suited for agile development environments where requirements are subject to change, as they allow developers to iterate quickly and adapt data models on the fly without schema migrations.Hierarchical Data: Document databases excel at storing hierarchical or nested data structures, such as JSON or XML documents, where relationships between entities are naturally represented as nested objects or arrays.A Time-Series Database (TSDB) is a type of database optimized for handling time-stamped or time-series data, where each data point is associated with a timestamp indicating when it was recorded. Here’s an introduction to TSDBs along with some key characteristics and use cases:: TSDBs are designed to efficiently store, retrieve, and analyze data points that are indexed by time.: They are optimized for ingesting large volumes of data points in real-time or at high frequencies, making them suitable for applications that generate continuous streams of data.Efficient Time-Based Queries: TSDBs provide fast querying capabilities to retrieve data based on time ranges, enabling time-based aggregations, filtering, and downsampling.Compression and Downsampling: To handle large datasets efficiently, TSDBs often support compression techniques and downsampling algorithms to reduce storage requirements without sacrificing data fidelity.: TSDBs are designed to scale horizontally to accommodate growing data volumes and increasing query loads, allowing for distributed storage and processing.Monitoring and Observability: TSDBs are commonly used for collecting and analyzing metrics, logs, and events from various sources such as IT infrastructure, applications, sensors, and IoT devices. They enable real-time monitoring, anomaly detection, and troubleshooting.Financial Analytics: TSDBs are utilized in financial services for storing and analyzing time-series data related to stock prices, market trends, trading volumes, and transaction histories. They support backtesting, risk analysis, and algorithmic trading strategies.Here’s a brief overview of each AWS database service and its use case:: A MySQL and PostgreSQL-compatible relational database built for the cloud, offering high performance and scalability. Use cases include OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) workloads, e-commerce applications, and SaaS platforms.: A fully managed data warehousing service that allows you to run complex analytical queries on large datasets. Ideal for data warehousing, business intelligence, and analytics applications.: A fully managed NoSQL database service that provides single-digit millisecond latency at any scale. Best suited for applications requiring low-latency data access, such as gaming, ad tech, and real-time bidding platforms.: A fully managed in-memory caching service compatible with Redis and Memcached, enabling you to deploy and manage distributed caches in the cloud. Commonly used to improve application performance by caching frequently accessed data. (for Apache Cassandra): A scalable, highly available, and fully managed Cassandra-compatible database service. Ideal for applications that require a wide-column store with high availability, such as IoT data processing and real-time analytics.: A fully managed time-series database service designed for IoT, telemetry, and operational applications. It can efficiently store and analyze time-series data at scale, making it suitable for monitoring, IoT, and industrial applications.Amazon Quantum Ledger Database (QLDB): A fully managed ledger database service that provides a transparent, immutable, and cryptographically verifiable transaction log. Use cases include auditing, compliance, and supply chain tracking where data integrity and immutability are critical.A fully managed graph database service that allows you to build and run graph applications. Ideal for applications requiring highly connected data models, such as social networks, recommendation engines, and fraud detection.: A fully managed MongoDB-compatible document database service that provides scalability, performance, and availability. Best suited for applications that require the flexibility of a document database with the reliability and scalability of AWS.Amazon OpenSearch Service: A fully managed service that makes it easy to deploy, operate, and scale Elasticsearch for log analytics, full-text search, and real-time application monitoring. Use cases include log analytics, search engines, and monitoring solutionsAmazon S3 (Simple Storage Service): A scalable object storage service designed for data storage and retrieval. While not a database service, S3 is commonly used for storing large volumes of unstructured data, such as images, videos, and backups.Each of these services caters to different data storage and processing requirements, so the choice depends on factors such as data volume, access patterns, query complexity, and scalability needs.

item.title: Building Enterprise Python Microservices with FastAPI in 2025 (7/10): Unit- & Integration Testing
item.author: Asbjorn Bering
item.content:encoded:
Building an enterprise-grade FastAPI microservice architecture — uv, Docker, Kafka, Debezium, PostgreSQL, Pytest, DynamoDB (AWS), IAM (AWS), TerraformIn the previous articles we’ve focused on developing our microservice, following the Repository Pattern. We have achieved loose coupling wrt. database implementation, making it possible to easily swap out databases down the line. Another great thing about loose coupling, is when it comes to writing unit tests for our service, as you shall see in this article.In addition to unit testing, we’ll also focus on integration testing our microservice in isolation — not distributed integration testing across multiple microservices. Since this series is about building an enterprise-grade microservice, we’ll focus on isolated tests; distributed tests are out of scope for now.We’re now adding our testsfolder. For reference, here’s the updated project structure. In this article we are going through everything that happens in our tests folder, and how we can extend our Dockerfile.dev to run tests automatically.├── Dockerfile.dev├── Dockerfile.prod├── docker-compose.yaml├── src│ ├── db│ │ ├── db_context.py│ │ └── settings.py│ ├── exceptions.py│ ├── logging_config.py│ ├── main.py│ │ ├── __init__.py│ │ ├── correlation_context.py│ │ └── correlation_id_middleware.py│ │ ├── implementations│ │ │ ├── AWS_DynamoDB│ │ │ │ ├── __init__.py│ │ │ │ ├── awsdynamodb_UserRepository.py│ │ │ │ └── utils.py│ │ │ └── PostgreSQL│ │ │ ├── __init__.py│ │ │ ├── debezium_config.py│ │ │ ├── models│ │ │ │ ├── ORM_User.py│ │ │ │ └── __init__.py│ │ │ └── postgres_UserRepository.py│ │ ├── __init__.py│ │ └── interface_UserRepository.py│ ├── routes│ │ ├── UserController.py│ │ └── __init__.py│ │ ├── UserSchemas.py│ └── service│ ├── UserService.py│ ├── __init__.py├── tests│ ├── integration│ │ ├── conftest.py│ └── unit│ ├── repository│ │ ├── __init__.py│ │ └── test_postgres_UserRepository.py│ ├── test_user_controller.py│ └── test_user_service.pyFirst we are going to look at our unit tests, and we are going to start from the controller layer, then the service layer, and finally the repository layer./tests/unit/test_user_controller.pyfrom src.dependencies import get_user_serviceimport pytestfrom fastapi.testclient import TestClientfrom unittest.mock import AsyncMockdef mock_user_service(): """Create a mock UserService for dependency injection.""" mock_service = AsyncMock()def sample_user_response_inactive(): """Create a sample inactive UserResponse""" from src.schemas import UserSchemas return UserSchemas.UserResponse( email="test@example.com", )# Tests for get_user method@pytest.mark.asyncioasync def test_get_user_success(mock_user_service, sample_user_response_inactive): """Test successful user retrieval.""" # Setup mock to return a user mock_user_service.get_user.return_value = sample_user_response_inactive # Override the dependency app.dependency_overrides[get_user_service] = lambda: mock_user_service # Make the request to the endpoint with TestClient(app) as client: response = client.get("/users", params={"email": "test@example.com"}) app.dependency_overrides.clear() # Verify the service method was called correctly mock_user_service.get_user.assert_called_once_with(email=sample_user_response_inactive.email) assert response.status_code == 200 user_data = response.json() assert user_data["email"] == sample_user_response_inactive.email assert user_data["is_active"] == sample_user_response_inactive.is_activeIn this test we are using two to:Mock a UserService objectReturn a UserResponse model.These can be used throughout our tests. I will now go through what happens, step by step, in our first unit test.Our mock UserService instance mock_user_service is going to return our UserResponse model, when mock_user_service.get_user is called.Since our controller on get_user_service , we must now override this dependency, such that it now depends on our mock_user_service .We make the request to the endpoint, and since we are now using our mock_user_service , we return the UserResponse defined in our when mock_user_service.get_user(email='test@example.com') is called.We clear the so that after the test, the controller depends on the real UserService again, not our mock.We assert that the get_user methods was called exactly once as expected.We assert that the response is as expected.This example shows how we can test our controller, by mocking dependencies (get_user_service ), Objects (UserService ), and object methods ( UserService.get_user )./tests/unit/test_user_service.pyimport pytestfrom unittest.mock import AsyncMock, patchdef user_service(): """Create a UserService with a mocked repository.""" from src.service.UserService import UserService from src.repository.interfaces.interface_UserRepository import UserRepository # Create a mock repository directly mock_repo = AsyncMock(spec=UserRepository) # Return the service with the mock repo return UserService(user_repository=mock_repo)def sample_user_inactive_nopw(): """Create a sample User (result from user_repo.get_user) for testing.""" from src.schemas import UserSchemas email="test@example.com", )# Tests for get_user method@pytest.mark.asyncioasync def test_get_user_success( user_service, sample_user_inactive_nopw ): """Test successful user retrieval.""" # Setup mock to return a User user_service.user_repository.get_user = AsyncMock(return_value=sample_user_inactive_nopw) # Call the method - returns UserResponse user = await user_service.get_user(sample_user_inactive_nopw.email) # Verify the repository method was called correctly user_service.user_repository.get_user.assert_called_once_with(sample_user_inactive_nopw.email) assert user.email == sample_user_inactive_nopw.email assert user.is_active is FalseNow we are testing the service layer, and as you can see, we follow the same principles from before. The key point here is the effect of the Repository Pattern: we’re now mocking the interface, not the actual implementation. This means that we do not have to change our unit tests for our service layer, when we swap database implementations./tests/unit/repository/test_postgres_repository.pyimport pytestfrom unittest.mock import AsyncMock, MagicMock, patchfrom sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSessionfrom sqlalchemy.exc import IntegrityError@pytest.fixture """Create a mock AsyncSession for testing.""" mock = AsyncMock(spec=AsyncSession) # Setup the async context manager for begin() context_manager = AsyncMock() mock.begin.return_value = context_managerdef user_repo(mock_db): """Create a UserRepository instance with a mock db.""" from src.repository.implementations.PostgreSQL.postgres_UserRepository import UserRepository return UserRepository(db=mock_db)def db_user(): """Create a sample UserORM object that would be returned from the database.""" from src.repository.implementations.PostgreSQL.models.ORM_User import UserORM user = MagicMock(spec=UserORM) user.email = "test@example.com" user.hashed_password = "hashed_password_value"# Tests for get_user method@pytest.mark.asyncioasync def test_get_user_success(user_repo, mock_db, db_user): """Test successful user retrieval.""" # Import inside test function to avoid early import from src.schemas import UserSchemas # Setup mock to return a user mock_result = MagicMock() mock_result.scalar_one_or_none.return_value = db_user mock_db.execute.return_value = mock_result user = await user_repo.get_user("test@example.com") assert user.email == "test@example.com" assert user.is_active is True mock_db.execute.assert_called_once()Here we are testing our actual PostgreSQL implementation, by mocking the PostgreSQL database. The context_manager is used for testing database , which we’ll use later on in this series. A database transaction is simply when multiple database operations are executed, and if one fails, all subsequent operations are cancelled, and all previous operations are rolled back. Also note that we are mocking the database response using UserORM to return our db_user as defined in the . This means when we call mock_db.execute we return our db_user . This in turn means that get_user translates this UserORM object into a User defined by our .In our unit tests it should now be clear that we are benefitting from the Repository Pattern, and that due to the loose coupling measures we’ve made earlier, we can very easily write unit tests for our service layer without worrying about the actual database implementation.For integration tests, we necessarily depend on our actual database implementations, since we’re testing the system end-to-end. We are not going to set up a test database, which is obviously best practise, since we want to make real database operations. However, its been chosen to implement our tests in a way that would easily allows us to use a test database.For this reason we are going to override our db_context that you might recall from our previous article. The db_context was our dependency function returning either an AsyncSession or a DynamoDBClient, dependent on whether our Settings were set to use either of those as its database backend. For the purpose of this article, we are simple going to focus on our PostgreSQL implementation./tests/integration/conftest.pyimport pytest(...)settings = get_settings()# Define override_db_context at module level - will be set properly laterasync def override_db_context():if settings.DATABASE_TYPE == DatabaseType.POSTGRES: test_engine = create_async_engine(settings.POSTGRES_DATABASE_URL_FOR_TESTING, echo=True) TestAsyncSessionLocal = async_sessionmaker( expire_on_commit=False, ) @pytest.fixture(scope="session") async def setup_database(): """Set up the test database.""" async with test_engine.begin() as conn: await conn.run_sync(Base.metadata.drop_all) await conn.run_sync(Base.metadata.create_all) # Clear tables instead of dropping them async with test_engine.begin() as conn: for table in reversed(Base.metadata.sorted_tables): await conn.execute(text('TRUNCATE TABLE auth.users CASCADE')) async def db_session(setup_database): """Create a fresh database session for each test.""" connection = await test_engine.connect() transaction = await connection.begin() session = AsyncSession(bind=connection, expire_on_commit=False) yield session await session.close() await transaction.rollback() await connection.close() # Override the module-level function async def postgresql_context(): async with TestAsyncSessionLocal() as session: yield session # Set the module-level function override_db_context = postgresql_contextelif settings.DATABASE_TYPE == DatabaseType.DYNAMODB: # Set the module-level function override_db_context = dynamodb_context raise ValueError(f"Invalid DATABASE_TYPE: {settings.DATABASE_TYPE}. Must be one of {[e.value for e in DatabaseType]}")@pytest.fixtureasync def async_client(setup_database): """Create an async client for testing the API.""" # Override the db_context dependency app.dependency_overrides[db_context] = override_db_context transport = ASGITransport(app=app) async with AsyncClient(transport=transport, base_url="http://test", follow_redirects=True) as client: app.dependency_overrides.clear()conftest.py configures our testing environment for our integration tests. So going through it step by step, if our database backend is PostgreSQL:We create an and a , just like we do in our start-up tasks. This is also where we allow to use another database, e.g. one specifically for testing.The setup_database fixture clears auth.users table prior to running tests.The db_session fixture creates a new per request.The postgresql_context acts as our new db_context; we override the original dependency with this context for testing. . That means when we the postgresql_context into our get_user_repository , and thus we use the our test database when testing our service.Finally, in async_client , we make the override and yield our client. This is AsyncClient is then the one we use to make test requests to our API, as you shall see in our actual integration tests. Finally, after each test, we remove our dependency override, so that we no longer depend on our testing context./tests/integration/test_api.pyimport pytestfrom httpx import AsyncClient@pytest.mark.asyncio(loop_scope="session")async def test_get_nonexistent_user(async_client: AsyncClient): """Test retrieving a user that doesn't exist""" "email": "nonexistent@example.com" response = await async_client.get( "/users", ) assert response.status_code == 404 data = response.json() assert data["error"] == f"User with email {test_user_data['email']} not found"Here we use the configurations made in conftest.py from before, to make actual API request, and thus test our service. In this example, we’re testing whether our service returns the expected error when a non-existent user is requested.Now, we want to run these files in our container. In order to do so we are going to build on top of our previous Dockerfile, where we were heavily inspired by uv’s multistage.Dockerfile . For simplicity, we are going to break the Dockerfile into logical bits.############################################ BUILDER stage###########################################FROM ghcr.io/astral-sh/uv:python3.12-bookworm-slim AS builderENV UV_COMPILE_BYTECODE=1 UV_LINK_MODE=copy# Disable Python downloads, because we want to use the system interpreter# across both images. If using a managed Python version, it needs to be# copied from the build image into the final image; see `standalone.Dockerfile`# for an example.ENV UV_PYTHON_DOWNLOADS=0RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/uv \ --mount=type=bind,source=uv.lock,target=uv.lock \ --mount=type=bind,source=pyproject.toml,target=pyproject.toml \ uv sync --frozen --no-install-project --no-devADD . /appRUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/uv \ uv sync --frozen --no-dev############################################ UNIT TEST stage###########################################FROM python:3.12-slim-bookworm AS unit-test# It is important to use the image that matches the builder, as the path to the# Python executable must be the same, e.g., using `python:3.11-slim-bookworm`# Copy the application from the builderCOPY --from=builder --chown=app:app /app /app# Place executables in the environment at the front of the pathENV PATH="/app/.venv/bin:$PATH"# Run unit tests by default (using config from pyproject.toml)CMD ["python", "-m", "pytest", "app/tests/unit"]We simply run our unit tests with the command python -m pytest app/tests/unit. Obviously we do not have to have our service running to run unit tests, as we are not making actual requests.############################################ INTEGRATION TEST stage###########################################FROM python:3.12-slim-bookworm AS integration-test# Copy the application from the builderCOPY --from=builder --chown=app:app /app /app# Install bash for scriptingRUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y bashENV PATH="/app/.venv/bin:$PATH"# Create test runner scriptRUN echo '#!/bin/bash
\echo "👉 Starting server..."
\uvicorn src.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 & 
\n\echo "👉 Waiting for server to start..."
\sleep 5
\echo "👉 Running integration tests..."
\python -m pytest app/tests/integration
\TEST_EXIT_CODE=$?
\echo "👉 Stopping server..."
\n' > /app/run_integration_tests.sh && chmod +x /app/run_integration_tests.sh# Run integration tests by defaultCMD ["/app/run_integration_tests.sh"]To run our integration tests, we are first starting our server, and then running the tests. This is obviously necessary because our integration tests obviously need our server to be running to send requests. For this we are making a short script to be executed on container start:We run the integration testsYou can find the actual Dockerfile here, as it will also include a stage that runs both unit- and integration tests.While this article focused more on making tests “work right” than on writing the “right” tests, it’s still important to consider which tests are most critical for your application. Ideally, we would have 100% code coverage, but oftentimes we want ship software before that happens — if it ever happens.From our growing Project Architecture I hope you can see how important it is, to consider everything from using the right patterns to putting stuff in the right place — you can easily give yourself and others a hard time maintaining even a simple application. Also, our microservice’s core functionality has barely changed since the start of this series-which highlights the big gap between a school-project FastAPI app and an enterprise-grade FastAPI service.In the next articles we are changing focus, as we will now focus on making our microservice work in a system of microservices. Up until now, we have made our service work as a single service, but in any microservice architecture, you’ll have multiple microservices . So in the following articles we shall discover how we can use event-driven architecture to make multiple loosely coupled microservices work together. Microservices obviously be loosely coupled, because not being defeats the whole point — then we should have a .

item.title: Building Enterprise Python Microservices with FastAPI in 2025 (8/10): SAGA- and Outbox Pattern
item.author: Asbjorn Bering
item.content:encoded: Building an enterprise-grade FastAPI microservice architecture — uv, Docker, Kafka, Debezium, PostgreSQL, Pytest, DynamoDB (AWS), IAM (AWS), TerraformBefore we make the actual implementation, we are going to discuss how we can achieve loose coupling between microservices using event-driven architecture. First we are going to introduce some relevant topics to make a general understanding.Central Concepts in Event-Driven ArchitectureA is central to a pub/sub system: it receives incoming published by and routes them to the appropriate , which can subscribe to.An typically consists of a and a . (Events can have additional metadata, but for simplicity, we’ll focus on these two parts.) The topic defines which the event goes into, i.e. a consumer will to specific topics and the is the data related to the event.For example, when we create a subscription, we might publish an event with the topic created_subscription_success and the new subscription data (such as subscription_id and email) as the payload. Now other services these created_subscription_success events, will now be able to react to the creation of a subscription. For example we our User microservice consumes this event, and creates a new user.In an event-driven system, a (or ) is an entity that sends events to the broker. A to a and processes incoming events.A is defined by a and a . When a broker receives an event, it places the event into all queues matching the event’s topic. This means we can have multiple queues representing the same topic, but with different group IDs. The purpose of the group ID is then to ensure:multiple instances of the same service are running, only one instance processes each event. This prevents duplicate processing and potential conflicts. The group ID ensures that all instances of the same service, subscribe to the same queue, making it impossible to pull the same event twice.If multiple different services subscribe to the same topic, each service receives its own copy of the event. The group ID allows each service to have its own queue for the topic, and the broker ensures the event is delivered to all relevant queues.In event-driven systems we face some common challenges: The dual write problem, and distributed transactions.The core idea of event-driven architecture is that a microservice performs an action and then informs other microservices about it, so they can react accordingly. A simple example is when our User Service create a user, our Subscription Service must create a subscription. The problem arises if our Subscription Service fails to create the subscription: we end up with an inconsistent state, where a user exists but their subscription does not. This is where the SAGA Choreography pattern becomes relevant: it enables us to roll back previous operations across microservices, helping to solve the distributed transaction problem.The dual write problem occurs when we want to update two external systems atomically. For example, we might want to write to the database and publish an event to the broker as a single, reliable operation. An example could be we want to create a subscription, and publish a created_subscription_success event. The problem occurs when one of the operations fail, i.e. we successfully create the subscription, but we are unable to publish created_subscription_success event — this could simply be because the broker is down. This is where the Outbox Table pattern becomes relevant, as it ensures this behaviour does not occur.SAGA Choreography PatternPut simply, in the SAGA Choreography pattern each local transaction publishes domain events, which then trigger related transactions in other services. This means we can achieve rollbacks by publishing events that trigger compensating actions in other services. For example, if our User Service publishes a user_created_from_new_subscription_failed event, our Subscription Service can consume this event and delete the related subscription, effectively rolling back the previous insertion. This way we ensure database consistency across multiple services.To address the dual write problem, the Outbox Table pattern introduces a dedicated outbox table in the database to store events. This way we can make a database operation and write to the outbox table in atomic manner. We then use (Change Data Capture) to read from the outbox table and ensure each event is published to the broker. This way we ensure that each database operation has a related event in the outbox table. If CDC fails to publish an event, we can implement retry logic or compensating actions to maintain consistency. Therefore we ensure consistency across our two systems by implementing the outbox pattern.In this article, we explored how to achieve loose coupling between microservices using event-driven architecture. We covered common challenges and how patterns like SAGA Choreography and Outbox Table can address them. In the next article, we’ll dive into the actual implementation with Kafka and Debezium in our FastAPI microservice architecture.

item.title: Building Enterprise Python Microservices with FastAPI in 2025 (9/10): Debezium & Outbox Table…
item.author: Asbjorn Bering
item.content:encoded: Building Enterprise Python Microservices with FastAPI in 2025 (9/10): Debezium & Outbox Table PatternBuilding an enterprise-grade FastAPI microservice architecture — uv, Docker, Kafka, Debezium, PostgreSQL, Pytest, DynamoDB (AWS), IAM (AWS), TerraformIn the previous article we discussed event-driven architecture to make our microservices loosely coupled. We also discussed common challenges and patterns to address these. In this article we are going to implement the SAGA Choreography pattern and the Outbox Table pattern using as our broker and for CDC. This is a common enterprise-grade stack when dealing with PostgreSQL database backends.Debezium is not compatible with DynamoDB, so for CDC with DynamoDB, we would use DynamoDB Streams in combination with AWS Lambda, for event distribution we could then use Kafka or SNS and SQS. In this article, we focus on the Kafka and Debezium stack with PostgreSQL.Outbox Table Definition & OutboxORMFirstly we are going to define our table, and the relating ORM.-- Create the schema if not existsCREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS auth;-- Create the users tableCREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS auth.users ( email VARCHAR(50) PRIMARY KEY, hashed_password VARCHAR(200), -- hashed (including salt));-- create the users outbox tableCREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS auth.users_outbox ( aggregatetype TEXT NOT NULL, aggregateid TEXT NOT NULL, eventtype TEXT NOT NULL, created_at BIGINT NOT NULLThe outbox table consist of the following: — unique id for each record — used as topic suffix — user id, in our case the user’s email — type of event, events with the same topic might have different event types. This way consumers can subscribe to one topic, and handle events differently based on the event type. — the data related to the event as JSON. — UNIX timestamp in millisecondsThis table definition is highly dependent on our choice of tool for CDC. In our case this table definition is heavily inspired by the official Debezium documentation./src/repository/implementations/PostgreSQL/models/ORM_User.pyimport uuid6from datetime import datetime ,timezonefrom sqlalchemy import Column, String, Boolean, BigInteger, JSONfrom sqlalchemy.dialects.postgresql import UUIDfrom sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_baseBase = declarative_base() __tablename__ = "users" __table_args__ = {"schema": "auth"} email = Column(String, primary_key=True, unique=True, index=True, nullable=False) hashed_password = Column(String, nullable=True) is_active = Column(Boolean, default=True)class UsersOutboxORM(Base): __tablename__ = "users_outbox" __table_args__ = {"schema": "auth"} # Specifies the schema id = Column(UUID(as_uuid=True), primary_key=True, default=uuid6.uuid6) aggregatetype = Column(String, nullable=False) # e.g., "user" -> TOPIC aggregateid = Column(String, nullable=False) # e.g., user_id eventtype = Column(String, nullable=False) # e.g., "user_created_success" payload = Column(JSON, nullable=False) # Event data as JSON created_at = Column(BigInteger, nullable=False, default=lambda: int(datetime.now(timezone.utc).timestamp() * 1000))Similar to how we created our UserORM , we must also define a UserOutboxORM to interact with our Outbox table in a pythonic manner. Note that we are using default values for id and created_at , as these should just be set automatically when we create a record./src/schemas/UserSchemas.py:(...) email: str hashed_password: Optional[str] = None is_active: Optional[bool] = Noneclass UserResponse(BaseModel): email: str is_active: Optional[bool] = None aggregatetype: str eventtype_prefix: strWe also create our Outbox model, as we are now not only creating a User , but also an Outbox record when we insert a User in our database./src/service/UserService.py(...) def __init__(self, user_repository: interface_UserRepository): self.user_repository = user_repository self, User_instance: UserSchemas.User, eventtype_prefix: str, payload_add: Dict[str, Any] = None ) -> None: await self.user_repository.create_user( User_instance = User_instance, Outbox_instance = UserSchemas.Outbox( aggregatetype = "user", aggregateid = User_instance.email, eventtype_prefix = eventtype_prefix, "email": User_instance.email, "is_active": True if User_instance.is_active else False, **(payload_add or {}) ) except ResourceAlreadyExistsException: raise logger.exception(f"Error creating user: {str(e)}") raise BaseAppException(f"Error creating user: {str(e)}") from eIn our create_user function we are now calling user.repository.create_user with not only the User instance as argument, but also an Outbox instance. This effectively tells our database backend, which records to put in the database./src/repository/implementations/PostgreSQL/postgres_UserRepository.py(...)class UserRepository(interface_UserRepository.UserRepository): def __init__(self, db: AsyncSession): self.db = db self, User_instance: UserSchemas.User, Outbox_instance: UserSchemas.Outbox db_user = UserORM( email=User_instance.email, hashed_password=User_instance.hashed_password, is_active=True if User_instance.is_active else False ) outbox_event = UsersOutboxORM( aggregatetype = Outbox_instance.aggregatetype, aggregateid = Outbox_instance.aggregateid, eventtype = f"{Outbox_instance.eventtype_prefix}_success", payload = Outbox_instance.payload ) async with self.db.begin(): # This ensures atomicity self.db.add(outbox_event) except IntegrityError as e: if "UniqueViolationError" in str(e.orig): logger.warning(f"User with email {User_instance.email} already exists") fail_event = UsersOutboxORM( aggregatetype = Outbox_instance.aggregatetype, aggregateid = Outbox_instance.aggregateid, eventtype = f"{Outbox_instance.eventtype_prefix}_failed", payload = Outbox_instance.payload.update({ "exception": "ResourceAlreadyExistsException" ) async with self.db.begin(): self.db.add(fail_event) raise ResourceAlreadyExistsException(f"User with email {User_instance.email} already exists") else: # Some other kind of IntegrityError (e.g., null value, foreign key constraint, etc) logger.exception(f"Error creating user: {str(e)}") fail_event = UsersOutboxORM( aggregatetype = Outbox_instance.aggregatetype, aggregateid = Outbox_instance.aggregateid, eventtype = f"{Outbox_instance.eventtype_prefix}_failed", payload = Outbox_instance.payload.update({ "exception": "BaseAppException" ) async with self.db.begin(): self.db.add(fail_event) raise BaseAppException(f"Database integrity error: {str(e)}") from e except ResourceAlreadyExistsException: raise logger.exception(f"Error creating user: {str(e)}") fail_event = UsersOutboxORM( aggregatetype = Outbox_instance.aggregatetype, aggregateid = Outbox_instance.aggregateid, eventtype = f"{Outbox_instance.eventtype_prefix}_failed", payload = Outbox_instance.payload.update({ "exception": "BaseAppException" ) async with self.db.begin(): self.db.add(fail_event) raise BaseAppException(f"Internal database error: {str(e)}") from eThis is where we actually write to our auth.users and auth.users_outbox in a transactional manner. We also write to auth.users_outbox table in the case where we fail to write to these tables. When we fail to write to our database, we append the exception to the payload. This means consumers of this event are able to handle these failures based on not only the event type, but also the exception when a fail event occurs. For example if we try to create a User that already exists, when our User Service consumes a subcription_created_success event, we do not want our Subscription Service to perform a roll back and delete the subscription. But if something else goes wrong, e.g. a database connection error, we would want a roll back action.We only need to care about writing to the Outbox table, because Debezium will take care of capturing the insertion and publish an event to Kafka.Now we have set up auth.users_outboxtable. In the next section we are going to configure Debezium, so we are publishing messages to Kafka, when records are inserted in the auth.users_outbox table.To make Debezium work, we must grant a database user the correct privileges, following the principle of least privilege.-- Start transaction for atomicityBEGIN;(...)CREATE ROLE debezium_user WITH REPLICATION LOGIN PASSWORD 'secure_debezium_password';-- Allow connection to the database(...)GRANT CONNECT ON DATABASE crypto_db TO debezium_user;-- Allow usage of the 'auth' schema(...)GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA auth TO debezium_user;-- Pre-create publication for Debezium (execute as superuser)CREATE PUBLICATION dbz_publication_user FOR TABLE auth.users_outbox;CREATE PUBLICATION dbz_publication_subscription FOR TABLE auth.subscriptions_outbox;-- Grant debezium_user minimal privileges needed for CDCGRANT SELECT ON TABLE auth.users_outbox TO debezium_user;GRANT SELECT ON TABLE auth.subscriptions_outbox TO debezium_user;COMMIT;We create the debezium_user with the REPLICATION attribute, allowing it to initiate logical replication connections and read the database’s Write-Ahead Log (WAL)-essential for Debezium’s CDC process.We allow debezium_user to connect to our database, crypto_db , and usage of the auth schema, which includes our auth.users_outbox table.We create a for each of the two outbox tables: auth.users_outbox and auth.subscriptions_outbox . PostgreSQL uses the concept of publications and subscriptions for logical replication. A publication is a set of tables whose changes are made available for replication. Debezium acts as a logical replication subscriber. It connects to the database, subscribes to these publications, and streams changes to downstream systems (like Kafka). By explicitly listing tables in the publication, we ensure only relevant data changes are exposed to Debezium, following the principle of least privilege.We grant debezium_user the ability to read (SELECT) from the auth.users_outbox and auth.subscriptions_outbox tables. Debezium needs to read the contents of these tables to capture and stream changes. Restricting privileges to only SELECT on these specific tables follows the principle of least privilege, reducing security risksNow we have created debezium_user , which is now able to capture changes to our Outbox tables.(...)class DatabaseType(str, Enum): POSTGRES = "postgres"class Settings(BaseSettings): DATABASE_TYPE: DatabaseType = DatabaseType.POSTGRES # Default to postgres # -------------------------------------------------------------------- # Postgres settings POSTGRES_DATABASE_URL: str = "postgresql+asyncpg://user:password@postgresql:5432/crypto_db" POSTGRES_DATABASE_URL_FOR_TESTING: str = "postgresql+asyncpg://postgres:password@postgresql:5432/crypto_db" # Debezium settings (only related to postgres) DB_HOST: str = "postgresql" DB_USER: str = "user_service_user" DB_PASSWORD: str = "super_secure_password" DB_NAME: str = "crypto_db" DEBEZIUM_URL: str = "http://debezium:8083/connectors" # -------------------------------------------------------------------- model_config = SettingsConfigDict( env_file = ".env", )def get_settings() -> Settings:In our Settings we are now storing connection configuration for our Debezium connector. We need this to create a Debezium on start up. Let’s go through our configuration to understand it better./src/repository/implementations/PostgreSQL/debezium_config.pyasync def generate_config_dict(settings): return { "name": "users-outbox-connector", "config": { "connector.class": "io.debezium.connector.postgresql.PostgresConnector", "tasks.max": "1", "database.hostname": settings.DB_HOST, "database.port": settings.DB_PORT, "database.user": settings.DB_USER, "database.password": settings.DB_PASSWORD, "database.dbname": settings.DB_NAME, "database.server.name": "userservice", "schema.include.list": "auth", "table.include.list": "auth.users_outbox", "plugin.name": "pgoutput", "publication.name": "dbz_publication_user", "tombstones.on.delete": "false", "topic.prefix": "userservice", "slot.name": "debezium_user", "transforms.outbox.type": "io.debezium.transforms.outbox.EventRouter", "transforms.outbox.route.by.field": "aggregatetype", "transforms.outbox.route.topic.replacement": "userservice.${routedByValue}", "transforms.outbox.field.event.id": "id", "transforms.outbox.field.event.key": "aggregateid", "transforms.outbox.field.event.type": "eventtype", "transforms.outbox.field.event.payload": "payload", "transforms.outbox.field.event.timestamp": "created_at", "transforms.outbox.field.event.timestamp.type": "io.debezium.time.Timestamp", "transforms.outbox.expand.json.payload": "true", "transforms.outbox.table.fields.additional.placement": "eventtype:envelope:type" }This function generates, using our Settings , our configuration. In our connector we use our debezium_user to connect to the database, and we subscribe to the dbz_publication_user to track insertions in auth.users_outbox . We configure Debezium’s EventRouter SMT to dynamically route events to Kafka topics based on the aggregatetype field (e.g., userservice.user), and include eventtype in the payload for downstream consumers to distinguish event types.For example, if we successfully insert the following record in auth.users :{ email: 'test@example.com', is_active: FalseWe would insert the following record in auth.users_outbox :{ id: 'some_unique_id', aggregateid: 'test@example.com', eventtype: 'user_created_from_new_subscription_success', payload: { email: 'test@example.com', is_active: False, subscription_id: 'unique_subscription_id' }, created_at = 14682387084298419 # dummy integer}And, with our configuration, this is the event our consumers will consume:ConsumerRecord( topic='userservice.user', offset=0, timestamp_type=0, key=b'{"schema":{"type":"string","optional":false},"payload":"dummy@email.com"}', value={ 'type': 'struct', {'type': 'string', 'optional': False, 'name': 'io.debezium.data.Json', 'version': 1, 'field': 'payload'}, {'type': 'string', 'optional': False, 'field': 'type'} ], 'name': 'userservice.auth.users_outbox.user.Value' 'payload': { 'payload': '{"email": "dummy@email.com", "is_active": False, "subscription_id": "unique_subscription_id"}', 'type': 'user_created_from_new_subscription_success' }, serialized_key_size=73, serialized_value_size=360, headers=(('id', b'1f027c97-d169-63a8-9cbf-56b86948d5eb'),)As shown, a consumer can use the eventtype and payload fields to determine the nature of the event (e.g., user_created_from_new_subcription_success) and access the relevant user data. For the purpose of this series, we are not going to dive deeper into specific Debezium configuration — we just need to be aware about the structure of the events we are going to consume to handle them properly.(...)async def lifespan(app: FastAPI): # Startup code (runs before application startup) logger.info("Running startup tasks...") settings = get_settings() if settings.DATABASE_TYPE == DatabaseType.POSTGRES: # Create an async engine engine = create_async_engine(settings.POSTGRES_DATABASE_URL, echo=True, future=True) # Create an async sessionmaker factory app.state.postgres_session = async_sessionmaker( expire_on_commit=False, # optional: objects stay active after commit ) # Create Debezium connector connector_config = await generate_config_dict( ) async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client: resp = await client.post(settings.DEBEZIUM_URL, json=connector_config) if resp.status_code not in {201, 409}: # Created (201) or Already Exists (409) raise RuntimeError(f"Failed to register Debezium connector: {resp.status_code} {resp.text}") logger.info("Startup tasks completed") yield # Shutdown code (runs after application shutdown) logger.info("Running shutdown tasks...") logger.info("Shutdown tasks completed") # This is where you put code that was previously in @app.on_event("shutdown")app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)When we start our application we make a POST request to Debezium, to create the defined in our configuration. If the connector is successfully created, it means Debezium now “listens” to insertions in the auth.users_outbox tables and publishes events, in the way we configured, to Kafka when insertions occur.This also means we have implemented the Outbox Table pattern using Debezium for CDC. Naturally, we will need to connect to a running instance of Debezium, which can be achieved using Docker. Check out my compose file for reference.This also wraps up how we can implement the Outbox Table Pattern when using PostgreSQL and Debezium for CDC. By using tools like Debezium, and not just developing our own CDC logic, we get a much more robust solution proven in other enterprise-grade applications.A good rule of thumb in a modern enterprise-grade stack is to look for popular external tools, before developing them yourself. Even though it introduces added complexity and more maintenance, it will almost always be a more robust and more secure solution.Now that we are able to publish messages to Kafka using the Outbox Table pattern, we are going to focus on consuming these messages in the next article. Additionally, we are going to use an example from my hobby project for reference, to see how our implementation of the SAGA Choreography- and Outbox Table patterns work.

item.title: The Complete Nginx Guide: From Basics to Advanced Configuration
item.author: Dominik
item.content:encoded: Nginx (pronounced “engine-x”) is a powerful, high-performance web server that has become the de facto standard for serving web applications. This guide will take you through everything you need to know about Nginx, from basic concepts to advanced configurations, with a special focus on PHP/Laravel applications.Nginx is an open-source web server that can also be used as a reverse proxy, load balancer, and HTTP cache. It was created by Igor Sysoev and publicly released in 2004. Nginx is known for its high performance, stability, rich feature set, simple configuration, and low resource consumption.High performance and low memory usageExcellent handling of concurrent connectionsBuilt-in load balancing capabilitiesExtensive documentation and community support: Configuration commands that control Nginx’s behavior: Blocks that group related directives: How Nginx handles connections: Web server configuration: Virtual host configuration: URL matching and handlingBasic Configuration Structure# Main contextuser www-data;error_log /var/log/nginx/error.log;events {}http { sendfile on; tcp_nodelay on; types_hash_max_size 2048; include /etc/nginx/mime.types; default_type application/octet-stream; # Logging access_log /var/log/nginx/access.log; error_log /var/log/nginx/error.log; server { server_name example.com; # Location context try_files $uri $uri/ /index.php?$query_string; }Installation and Basic SetupUbuntu/Debian Installation# Update package listsudo apt updatesudo apt install nginxsudo systemctl start nginx# Enable Nginx to start on bootsudo systemctl enable nginxsudo systemctl status nginxBasic Configuration File Structure/etc/nginx/├── nginx.conf # Main configuration file├── sites-available/ # Available site configurations├── sites-enabled/ # Enabled site configurations├── conf.d/ # Additional configuration files└── snippets/ # Reusable configuration snippetsMain Configuration (nginx.conf)# /etc/nginx/nginx.confuser www-data;pid /run/nginx.pid; worker_connections 1024;} # Basic Settings tcp_nopush on; keepalive_timeout 65; types_hash_max_size 2048; # MIME Types include /etc/nginx/mime.types; default_type application/octet-stream; access_log /var/log/nginx/access.log; error_log /var/log/nginx/error.log; # Gzip Settings gzip_vary on; gzip_comp_level 6; gzip_types text/plain text/css application/json application/javascript text/xml application/xml application/xml+rss text/javascript; # Virtual Host Configs include /etc/nginx/conf.d/*.conf; include /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/*;Virtual Host Configuration# /etc/nginx/sites-available/example.comserver { server_name example.com www.example.com; root /var/www/example.com/public; index index.php index.html; add_header X-Frame-Options "SAMEORIGIN"; add_header X-Content-Type-Options "nosniff"; add_header X-XSS-Protection "1; mode=block"; access_log /var/log/nginx/example.com.access.log; error_log /var/log/nginx/example.com.error.log; try_files $uri $uri/ /index.php?$query_string; fastcgi_pass unix:/var/run/php/php8.2-fpm.sock; fastcgi_param SCRIPT_FILENAME $document_root$fastcgi_script_name; }location /static/ { alias /var/www/static/; add_header Cache-Control "public, no-transform";upstream backend { server backend1.example.com:8080; server backend2.example.com:8080; server backend3.example.com:8080; location / { proxy_pass http://backend; proxy_set_header Host $host; proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr; }server { listen 443 ssl http2; ssl_certificate /etc/nginx/ssl/example.com.crt; ssl_certificate_key /etc/nginx/ssl/example.com.key; ssl_protocols TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3; ssl_ciphers ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384:ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384:ECDHE-ECDSA-CHACHA20-POLY1305:ECDHE-RSA-CHACHA20-POLY1305:DHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:DHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384; ssl_prefer_server_ciphers off; ssl_session_timeout 1d; ssl_session_cache shared:SSL:50m; ssl_session_tickets off; ssl_stapling_verify on; add_header Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=63072000" always;}1. Worker Processes and Connectionsworker_processes auto; # Automatically set based on CPU coresworker_rlimit_nofile 65535; # Increase file descriptor limit worker_connections 1024; use epoll; # Efficient event processing method# FastCGI Cachefastcgi_cache_path /tmp/nginx_cache levels=1:2 keys_zone=my_cache:10m max_size=10g inactive=60m use_temp_path=off; fastcgi_cache my_cache; fastcgi_cache_valid 200 60m; fastcgi_cache_use_stale error timeout http_500 http_503; fastcgi_cache_key "$request_method$request_uri";}Gzip compression reduces the size of files sent from the server to the client, which can significantly decrease the time it takes for a web page to load. This is especially beneficial for users with slower internet connections. By enabling gzip, you can improve the performance and speed of your website.: This header helps prevent clickjacking attacks by controlling whether a browser should be allowed to render a page in a <frame>, <iframe>, <embed>, or <object>. Setting it to SAMEORIGIN allows the page to be displayed only on the same origin as the page itself.: This header prevents browsers from MIME-sniffing a response away from the declared content-type. Setting it to nosniff helps prevent attacks based on MIME-type confusion.: This header enables the Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) filter built into most modern web browsers. Setting it to 1; mode=block will block the page if an XSS attack is detected.: This header controls how much referrer information should be included with requests. Setting it to strict-origin-when-cross-origin provides a good balance between security and usability.Client Body and Header TimeoutsThese settings control how long the server will wait for a client to send the body and headers of a request. By setting appropriate timeouts, you can prevent slow clients from holding connections open indefinitely, which can help protect against certain types of denial-of-service (DoS) attacks.Proxy Settings for WebSocketsWhen using Nginx as a reverse proxy for WebSocket connections, it’s important to set the appropriate headers and timeouts to ensure that the connection remains open and responsive. The proxy_set_header directives ensure that the correct headers are passed to the backend server, while the proxy_read_timeout, proxy_send_timeout, and proxy_connect_timeout settings help maintain a stable connection.1. Basic Security Headersadd_header X-Frame-Options "SAMEORIGIN" always;add_header X-XSS-Protection "1; mode=block" always;add_header X-Content-Type-Options "nosniff" always;add_header Referrer-Policy "no-referrer-when-downgrade" always;add_header Content-Security-Policy "default-src 'self' http: https: data: blob: 'unsafe-inline'" always;add_header Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains" always;limit_req_zone $binary_remote_addr zone=one:10m rate=1r/s; location /login { limit_req zone=one burst=5 nodelay; proxy_pass http://backend;}ssl_protocols TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3;ssl_prefer_server_ciphers on;ssl_ciphers ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384:ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384:ECDHE-ECDSA-CHACHA20-POLY1305:ECDHE-RSA-CHACHA20-POLY1305:DHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:DHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384;1. Standard Installation (Without Docker)# /etc/nginx/sites-available/laravelserver { server_name laravel.example.com; root /var/www/laravel/public; add_header X-Frame-Options "SAMEORIGIN"; add_header X-Content-Type-Options "nosniff"; charset utf-8; try_files $uri $uri/ /index.php?$query_string; location = /favicon.ico { access_log off; log_not_found off; } location = /robots.txt { access_log off; log_not_found off; } error_page 404 /index.php; fastcgi_pass unix:/var/run/php/php8.2-fpm.sock; fastcgi_param SCRIPT_FILENAME $realpath_root$fastcgi_script_name; include fastcgi_params; location ~ /\.(?!well-known).* { deny all;}# docker/nginx/conf.d/default.confserver { server_name _; index index.php index.html; client_max_body_size 100M; client_body_buffer_size 100M; client_body_timeout 60s; client_header_timeout 60s; send_timeout 60s; gzip on; gzip_min_length 10240; gzip_proxied expired no-cache no-store private auth; gzip_types text/plain text/css text/xml text/javascript application/x-javascript application/xml application/javascript; gzip_disable "MSIE [1-6]\."; add_header X-Frame-Options "SAMEORIGIN"; add_header X-Content-Type-Options "nosniff"; add_header X-XSS-Protection "1; mode=block"; add_header Referrer-Policy "strict-origin-when-cross-origin"; access_log /var/www/storage/logs/nginx_access.log; error_log /var/www/storage/logs/nginx_error.log; try_files $uri $uri/ /index.php?$query_string; location ^~ /storage/ { alias /var/www/storage/app/public/; try_files $uri $uri/ =404; add_header Cache-Control "public, no-transform"; add_header X-Content-Type-Options "nosniff"; } location /build/ { alias /var/www/public/build/; add_header Cache-Control "public, max-age=31536000, immutable"; add_header X-Content-Type-Options "nosniff"; try_files $uri =404; location ~ \.php$ { fastcgi_index index.php; fastcgi_param SCRIPT_FILENAME $document_root$fastcgi_script_name; fastcgi_param PATH_INFO $fastcgi_path_info; fastcgi_buffers 16 16k; fastcgi_read_timeout 600; fastcgi_send_timeout 600; } location ~* \.(js|css|png|jpg|jpeg|gif|ico|svg|woff|woff2|ttf|eot)$ { log_not_found off; add_header Cache-Control "public, max-age=31536000, immutable"; add_header X-Content-Type-Options "nosniff"; # Deny access to sensitive files location ~ /\.(?!well-known).* { } deny all; deny all;}Common Issues and SolutionsCheck if PHP-FPM is runningVerify socket file existsVerify root directory pathEnsure try_files directive is correct# Fix permissions for Laravel sudo chown -R www-data:www-data /var/www/laravel sudo chmod -R 755 /var/www/laravel sudo chmod -R 775 /var/www/laravel/storage sudo chmod -R 775 /var/www/laravel/bootstrap/cache# Test configurationnginx -tsudo systemctl reload nginxtail -f /var/log/nginx/error.logtail -f /var/log/nginx/access.logNginx is a powerful and flexible web server that can handle various use cases, from serving static files to complex load balancing scenarios. By following the best practices outlined in this guide, you can create a secure, performant, and maintainable web server configuration for your PHP/Laravel applications.Always test configurations before applying themRegularly update Nginx and its modulesBack up your configurationsBasic authentication can be used to restrict access to certain parts of your website. To set up basic authentication in Nginx, follow these steps:Use the htpasswd command to create a password file:sudo htpasswd -c /etc/nginx/.htpasswd usernameYou will be prompted to enter a password for the user.Update Nginx ConfigurationAdd the following lines to your server block in the Nginx configuration:location / { auth_basic "Restricted Content"; auth_basic_user_file /etc/nginx/.htpasswd; try_files $uri $uri/ /index.php?$query_string;After updating the configuration, reload Nginx to apply the changes:sudo systemctl reload nginxThis setup will prompt users to enter a username and password when accessing the restricted area.To use the htpasswd command for creating password files, you need to install the apache2-utils package. This can be done with the following command:sudo apt install apache2-utilsHandling .htpasswd in DockerTo generate the .htpasswd file during the build, you can add a command like this to your Dockerfile:htpasswd -bc /etc/nginx/.htpasswd username passwordReplace username and password with your desired credentials. This command installs apache2-utils and creates the .htpasswd file with the specified user credentials.
