item.title: Introducing the AWS Product Lifecycle page and AWS service availability updates
item.author: Sébastien Stormacq
item.content:encoded: Today, we’re introducing the AWS Product Lifecycle page, a centralized resource that provides comprehensive information about service availability changes across AWS.The new AWS Product Lifecycle page consolidates all service availability information in one convenient location. This dedicated resource offers detailed visibility into three key categories of changes: 1) services closing access to new customers, 2) services that have announced end of support, and 3) services that have reached their end of support date. For each service listed, you can access specific end-of-support dates, recommended migration paths, and links to relevant documentation, enabling more efficient planning for service transitions.The AWS Product Lifecycle page helps you stay informed about changes that may affect your workloads and enables more efficient planning for service transitions. The centralized nature of this resource reduces the time and effort needed to track service lifecycle information, allowing you to focus more on your core business objectives and less on administrative overhead.Today on the new Product Lifecycle page, you will see updates about the following changes to services and capabilities:After careful consideration, we’re announcing availability changes for a select group of AWS services and features. We understand that the decision to end support for a service or feature significantly impacts your operations. We approach such decisions only after thorough evaluation, and when end of support is necessary, we provide detailed guidance on available alternatives and comprehensive support for migration.Services closing access to new customersWe’re closing access to new customers after June 20, 2025, for the following services or capabilities listed. Existing customers will be able to continue to use the service.Services that have announced end of supportThe following services will no longer be supported. To find out more about service specific end-of-support dates, as well as detailed migration information, please visit individual service documentation pages.Services that have reached their end of supportThe following services have reached their end of support date and can no longer be accessed:The AWS Product Lifecycle page is available and all the changes described in this post are listed on the new page now. We recommend that you bookmark this page and check out What’s New with AWS? for upcoming AWS service availability updates. For more information about using this new resource, contact us or your usual AWS Support contacts for specific guidance on transitioning affected workloads.— seb

item.title: Exploring the latest features of the Amazon Q Developer CLI
item.author: Brian Beach
item.content:encoded: It’s been a few weeks since my last post about the Amazon Q Developer Command Line Interface (CLI), and I’m excited to share all the great new features and improvements the team has been working on. The CLI has been evolving rapidly with a focus on enhancing user experience, improving context management, and adding powerful new capabilities. In this post, I’ll walk you through the most significant changes that make the Amazon Q Developer CLI even more powerful and user-friendly.One of the most requested features has been the ability to persist conversations, and I’m thrilled to share that this is now available. With the new command, your conversations are now automatically saved by a working directory. This means you can pick up right where you left off when you return to a project, without having to rebuild context or repeat information.Q Developer has also added two new commands to give you more control over your conversation state: allows you to explicitly save the current conversation state lets you restore a previously saved conversationThese commands make it easier to manage multiple conversation threads related to different aspects of your project. You can save a conversation about one feature, switch to working on something else, and then load the previous conversation when you’re ready to continue.MCP and Tool Use EnhancementsFirst, Q Developer has implemented background MCP server loading, which significantly improves startup time for . Instead of waiting for all MCP servers to initialize before you can start interacting with Q Developer, the CLI now loads servers in the background while you begin your conversation. This means you can start working immediately, with tools becoming available as their servers finish loading.The team has also added a new subcommand, , which provides a dedicated interface for updating and managing your MCP server configuration. This makes it easier to add, remove, or modify the MCP servers that extend your CLI’s capabilities.For more granular control over which tools can be used, Q Developer has added the command in . This allows you to manage permissions for individual tools, giving you more control over what Q Developer can do in your environment. You can also reset permissions for a specific tool if you change your mind.Context is crucial for getting the most out of Q Developer, and the team has made several improvements to how you can manage and view context:The file selection in ‘s fuzzy finder is now git-aware, making it easier to include relevant files from your repository. This is particularly useful when working with large codebases, as it helps you focus on the files that matter for your current task.Q Developer has added fuzzy search for slash commands with , allowing you to quickly find and execute commands without remembering their exact syntax. This makes the CLI more accessible, especially for new users or those who don’t use certain commands frequently.The command has been improved to provide more detailed information about the current context, helping you understand what Q Developer knows about your environment. The team has also enhanced the context file display in to make it more informative and easier to read.One of the most exciting additions is the new capability for dynamically adding context to messages with context hooks. This allows the CLI to automatically include relevant context based on your conversation, improving the quality of responses without requiring manual context management.Context Window Awareness and OptimizationAs conversations grow longer, managing the context window becomes increasingly important. Q Developer has added two new commands to help with this: displays an estimate of the context window usage, helping you understand how much of the available context space you’re using summarizes the conversation history, allowing you to reduce the size of the context while preserving the important informationThese tools help you make the most of the available context window, ensuring that Q Developer has access to the most relevant information without running into token limits.I’m particularly excited to announce that now supports images! This opens up a whole new dimension of interaction, allowing you to share screenshots, diagrams, or other visual information with Q Developer. This can be incredibly useful for debugging UI issues, discussing design concepts, or explaining complex ideas that are difficult to convey through text alone.For complex queries or detailed instructions, you may want multiple paragraphs. Q Developer supports , allowing you to add a newline character to the prompt. In addition, the team has added the command, which opens your configured text editor for composing prompts. This makes it much easier to craft detailed, multi-paragraph prompts or to edit and refine your questions before sending them to Q Developer.I’m happy to announce that Q Developer has expanded its regional availability. Professional tier users can now access Q Developer in the Frankfurt region (eu-central-1). This expansion is part of Q Developer’s ongoing effort to provide lower latency and better service to customers across the globe. By adding support for the Frankfurt region, Amazon Q Developer is more accessible to European customers, allowing them to benefit from reduced latency and improved performance.Ability to Manage Issues in CLIThe command in allows you to create new GitHub issuesThe tool provides a programmatic way for Q Developer to help you create detailed issue reportsThese features streamline the feedback process, making it easier for you to report bugs or request features, and for the team to improve the CLI based on your input.Keeping Up with Future ChangesTo help you stay informed about new features and improvements, Q Developer has added a flag to the command. This displays the change log directly from the CLI, making it easy to see what’s new without having to visit the GitHub repository or read blog posts like this one.The Amazon Q Developer CLI continues to evolve rapidly, with new features and improvements that make it an even more powerful tool for developers. From conversation persistence to image support, these updates reflect Q Developer’s commitment to building a CLI that helps you be more productive and effective in your daily work. I encourage you to try out these new features by installing the Amazon Q Developer CLI. Thank you for your continued support and feedback, which helps make Amazon Q Developer better every day.

item.title: Join AWS Cloud Infrastructure Day to learn cutting-edge innovations building global cloud infrastructure
item.author: Channy Yun (윤석찬)
item.content:encoded: Join us for AWS Cloud Infrastructure Day, a free-to-attend one-day virtual event on May 22, 2025, starting at 11:00 AM PDT (2:00 PM EDT). We will stream the event simultaneously across multiple platforms, including LinkedIn Live, Twitter, YouTube, and Twitch.Here are some of the highlights you can expect from this event:Willem Visser, VP of EC2 Technology will open with the introduction of the AWS journey since 2006, when Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2) was launched with the goal of customer-obsessed innovation. He will speak about the progress made over nearly two decades in cloud infrastructure to support both startups and enterprise workloads based on scale, capacity, and flexibility.You can learn how AWS developed beyond computing instances to create a complete cloud infrastructure, including the parallel evolution of services like storage and networking capabilities.Todd Kennedy, Principal Engineer, GoDaddy, will share GoDaddy’s Graviton adoption journey and the benefits it reaped from Graviton. Todd will walk through an example to demonstrate moving Rust workloads to Graviton. Learn how GoDaddy achieved 40 percent compute cost savings and over 20 percent performance gains.This event covers a variety of topics related to AWS Cloud infrastructure. Here are interesting topics that caught my interest:Generative AI at the edge – You can learn how to select, fine-tune, and deploy small language models (SLMs) for on-premises and edge use cases due to data residency requirements using AWS hybrid and edge services.Serverless for agentic AI auditability – You can learn how AWS Step Functions and AWS Lambda transform opaque agentic AI system operations into transparent, auditable workflows. – You can get a close look at AWS innovation across silicon, server, and data centers and learn how customers are using AI chips. Learn how you can get started and reduce your generative AI costs. – You can learn how AWS infrastructure—from physical fiber to software-defined networking—enables unparalleled performance and reliability at global scale. The session covers modern application networking patterns while emphasizing secure connectivity solutions for hybrid environments.This event is perfect for technical decision-makers and developers and offers deep technical insights and hands-on demonstrations of the latest AWS Cloud infrastructure solutions.(This survey is hosted by an external company. AWS handles your information as described in the AWS Privacy Notice. AWS will own the data gathered via this survey and will not share the information collected with survey respondents.)

item.title: Amazon Inspector enhances container security by mapping Amazon ECR images to running containers
item.author: Elizabeth Fuentes
item.content:encoded: When running container workloads, you need to understand how software vulnerabilities create security risks for your resources. Until now, you could identify vulnerabilities in your Amazon Elastic Container Registry (Amazon ECR) images, but couldn’t determine if these images were active in containers or track their usage. With no visibility if these images were being used on running clusters, you had limited ability to prioritize fixes based on actual deployment and usage patterns.Starting today, Amazon Inspector offers two new features that enhance vulnerability management, giving you a more comprehensive view of your container images. First, Amazon Inspector now maps Amazon ECR images to running containers, enabling security teams to prioritize vulnerabilities based on containers currently running in your environment. With these new capabilities, you can analyze vulnerabilities in your Amazon ECR images and prioritize findings based on whether they are currently running and when they last ran in your container environment. Additionally, you can see the cluster Amazon Resource Name (ARN), number EKS pods or ECS tasks where an image is deployed, helping you prioritize fixes based on usage and severity.Through continual monitoring and tracking of images running on containers, Amazon Inspector helps teams identify which container images are actively running in their environment and where they’re deployed, detecting Amazon ECR images running on containers in Amazon Elastic Container Service (Amazon ECS) and Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (Amazon EKS), and any associated vulnerabilities. This solution supports teams managing Amazon ECR images across single AWS accounts, cross-account scenarios, and AWS Organizations with delegated administrator capabilities, enabling centralized vulnerability management based on container images running patterns.In the Amazon Inspector console, I navigate to and select from the navigation panel. Here, I can configure the new settings by choosing between and . I leave it as it is by default with and set the to 14 days. These settings make it so that Inspector monitors my images based on when they were running in the last 14 days in my Amazon ECS or Amazon EKS environments. After applying these settings, Amazon Inspector starts tracking information about images running on containers and incorporating it into vulnerability findings, helping me focus on images actively running in containers in my environment.After it’s configured, I can view information about images running on containers in the menu, where I can see last in-use and pull dates, along with EKS pods or ECS tasks count.When selecting the number of Deployed ECS Tasks/EKS Pods, I can see the cluster ARN, last use dates, and Type for each image.For cross-account visibility demonstration, I have a repository with EKS pods deployed in two accounts. In the menu, I navigate to , select my repository name and choose the . As before, I can see the number of deployed EKS pods/ECS tasks.When I select the number of deployed EKS pods/ECS tasks, I can see that it is running in a different account.In the Findings menu, I can review any vulnerabilities, and by selecting one, I can find the date and Deployed ECS Tasks/EKS Pods involved in the vulnerability under data, helping me prioritize remediation based on actual usage.In the menu, you can now search for vulnerabilities within account management, using filters such as , and .Monitoring based on container image lifecycle – Amazon Inspector now determines image activity based on: image push date ranging duration 14, 30, 60, 90, or 180 days or lifetime, image pull date from 14, 30, 60, 90, or 180 days, stopped duration from never to 14, 30, 60, 90, or 180 days and status of image running on the container. This flexibility lets organizations tailor their monitoring strategy based on actual container image usage rather than only repository events. For Amazon EKS and Amazon ECS workloads, last in use, push and pull duration are set to 14 days, which is now the default for new customers.Image runtime-aware finding details – To help prioritize remediation efforts, each finding in Amazon Inspector now includes the lastInUseAt date and InUseCount, indicating when an image was last running on the containers and the number of deployed EKS pods/ ECS tasks currently using it. Amazon Inspector monitors both Amazon ECR last pull date data and images running on Amazon ECS tasks or Amazon EKS pods container data for all accounts, updating this information at least once daily. Amazon Inspector integrates these details into all findings reports and seamlessly works with Amazon EventBridge. You can filter findings based on the lastInUseAt field using rolling window or fixed range options, and you can filter images based on their last running date within the last 14, 30, 60, or 90 days.Comprehensive security coverage – Amazon Inspector now provides unified vulnerability assessments for both traditional Linux distributions and minimal base images including scratch, distroless, and Chainguard images through a single service. This extended coverage eliminates the need for multiple scanning solutions while maintaining robust security practices across your entire container ecosystem, from traditional distributions to highly optimized container environments. The service streamlines security operations by providing comprehensive vulnerability management through a centralized platform, enabling efficient assessment of all container types.Enhanced cross-account visibility – Security management across single accounts, cross-account setups, and AWS Organizations is now supported through delegated administrator capabilities. Amazon Inspector shares images running on container information within the same organization, which is particularly valuable for accounts maintaining golden image repositories. Amazon Inspector provides all ARNs for Amazon EKS and Amazon ECS clusters where images are running, if the resource belongs to the account with an API, providing comprehensive visibility across multiple AWS accounts. The system updates deployed EKS pods or ECS tasks information at least one time daily and automatically maintains accuracy as accounts join or leave the organization.PS: Writing a blog post at AWS is always a team effort, even when you see only one name under the post title. In this case, I want to thank Nirali Desai, for her generous help with technical guidance, and expertise, which made this overview possible and comprehensive.(This survey is hosted by an external company. AWS handles your information as described in the AWS Privacy Notice. AWS will own the data gathered via this survey and will not share the information collected with survey respondents.)

item.title: AWS Weekly Roundup: Strands Agents, AWS Transform, Amazon Bedrock Guardrails, AWS CodeBuild, and more (May 19, 2025)
item.author: Danilo Poccia
item.content:encoded: Many events are taking place in this period! Last week I was at the AI Week in Italy. This week I’ll be in Zurich for the AWS Community Day – Switzerland. On May 22, you can join us remotely for AWS Cloud Infrastructure Day to learn about cutting-edge advances across compute, AI/ML, storage, networking, serverless technologies, and global infrastructure. Look for events near you for an opportunity to share your knowledge and learn from others.What got me particularly excited last Friday was the introduction of Strands Agents, an open source SDK that you can use to build and run AI agents in just a few lines of code. It can scale from simple to complex use cases, including local development and production deployment. By default, it uses Amazon Bedrock as model provider, but many others are supported, including Ollama (to run models locally), Anthropic, Llama API, and LiteLLM (to provide a unified interface for other providers such as Mistral). With Strands, you can use any Python function as a tool for your agent with the decorator. Strands provides many example tools for manipulating files, making API requests, and interacting with AWS APIs. You can also choose from thousands of published Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, including this suite of specialized MCP servers that help you get the most out of AWS. Multiple teams at AWS already use Strands for their AI agents in production, including Amazon Q Developer, AWS Glue, and VPC Reachability Analyzer. Read it all in Clare’s post. Here are the other launches that got my attention: Here are some additional projects, blog posts, and news items that you might find interesting:Check your calendars and sign up for these upcoming AWS events:AWS Cloud Infrastructure Day – On May 22, discover the latest innovations in AWS Cloud infrastructure technologies at this exclusive technical event.AWS re:Inforce – Mark your calendars for AWS re:Inforce (June 16–18) in Philadelphia, PA. AWS re:Inforce is a learning conference focused on AWS security solutions, cloud security, compliance, and identity.AWS Partners Events – You’ll find a variety of AWS Partner events that will inspire and educate you, whether you’re just getting started on your cloud journey or you’re looking to solve new business challenges.That’s all for this week. Check back next Monday for another Weekly Roundup!

item.title: Mastering Amazon Q Developer Part 1: Crafting Effective Prompts
item.author: Will Matos
item.content:encoded:
As organizations increasingly adopt AI-powered tools to enhance developer productivity, your ability to effectively communicate with these assistants becomes a valuable skill. This guide explores how you can craft prompts that deliver accurate, useful results when working with Amazon Q Developer.Your success with Amazon Q Developer depends directly on how well you communicate with it. Through my work as a Principal Specialist Solutions Architect on the Next Generation Developer Experience team at AWS, I’ve observed that developers experience varying degrees of success based primarily on their approach to prompt construction. The difference between a vague request and a well-structured prompt can be the difference between wasted time and a productivity breakthrough.Recent McKinsey research reveals that developers can complete tasks up to twice as fast with generative AI when using proper prompting techniques [1]. Even more impressive, developers tackling complex tasks are 25-30% more likely to complete them within given time-frames when using these tools effectively. These productivity gains aren’t automatic—they depend on mastering the art and science of prompt engineering.Based on patterns observed across numerous customer interactions, this guide provides practical techniques to help you maximize the value of your AI-assisted development experience. You’ll learn how to transform your interactions to consistently produce helpful, relevant assistance that can dramatically improve your development workflow.Structure your prompts with clear context, specific requirements, and desired output formatInclude relevant technical details about your environment and constraintsAvoid vague requests and provide specific examples when possibleUse the provided prompt template to ensure consistent resultsGetting Started with Amazon Q DeveloperAlready using Amazon Q Developer? Great! This guide will help you get more value from your interactions. If you haven’t set up Amazon Q Developer yet, check out the getting started guide.Understanding the Impact of Good PromptsThe rapid adoption of AI technologies makes prompt engineering skills essential for today’s developers. McKinsey’s latest global survey reveals that 65% of organizations regularly use generative AI, nearly double from their previous survey. When developers master prompt engineering, they’re 25-30% more likely to complete complex tasks within given timeframes.What Makes an Effective Prompt? State exactly what you need Describe your project, requirements, and constraints Provide code, configuration, or other additional context Specify how you want the information presentedHere’s how this works in practice:How do I deploy a container on AWS? I need to deploy a containerized Node.js e-commerce application that handles 50,000 daily users with peak loads during promotional events. Requirements: - High availability across multiple regions - MongoDB for persistence - Auto-scaling capabilities
Please provide: 1. AWS architecture diagram 2. List of required services with configurations 3. Security best practices 4. Operational monitoring recommendations Not much to go on here. Amazon Q Developer will likely provide generic documentation.Another vague prompt with a generic response.What's the best practice?The prompt is so vague that Amazon Q Developer responds by asking clarifying questions.The more specific prompt allows Amazon Q Developer to provide a more precise response. The quality of information you receive directly correlates with the quality of the information you provide.Proven Techniques for Better ResultsTo help you apply these principles consistently, I’ve developed a template structure that incorporates all the key elements of an effective prompt. This framework can be adapted for various scenarios and serves as a starting point for your interactions with Amazon Q Developer. While Amazon Q Developer will fill in some parts of this context (see the next post in this series), you just need to make sure this information is available.These are the principles demonstrated in the template:Technical Context RequirementsSpecify your technology stack and versionsInclude environment detailsMention compliance requirementsDefine scale expectationsInclude relevant code snippetsReference configuration filesShow current architectureRequest specific documentation formatsAsk for diagrams when neededSpecify code language preferencesIndicate level of detail neededThe specification of the output format ensure the response is what you expect.Quick Reference Prompt TemplateUse this template to structure your prompts: [Business Context] - Project description: - Performance requirements: - Compliance needs: - Scale expectations:
[Technical Details] - Current technology stack: - Versions/dependencies: - Technical constraints: - Environment details:
[Specific Request] - Task description: - Expected outcome: - Special considerations:
[Output Format] - Desired format: - Level of detail: - Examples needed: - Additional requirements:Best Practices for Daily UseSuccessfully working with Amazon Q Developer requires consistent application of proven practices. These guidelines, developed through extensive customer interactions, will help you maximize the value of your AI-assisted development experience.Start with clear business objectivesInclude relevant technical constraintsSpecify Request Provide when possibleThrough extensive customer interactions, we’ve found that following these practices consistently produces better results and reduces the need for follow-up clarification.In the next part of this series, we’ll explore advanced context management in Amazon Q Developer and dive into the new prompt catalog features. You’ll learn how to:Build and maintain context across multiple interactionsUse the prompt catalog effectivelyHandle complex, multi-step development tasksOptimize responses for your specific use casesStay tuned, and start applying these techniques today to transform how you build on AWS!

item.title: Enable experiment tracking for your next ML project with Cascade
item.author: Ilia Moiseev
item.content:encoded: Cascade is an open-source solution for small-scale MLOps. If you are a machine learning enthusiast, a single ML engineer or in a small team, Cascade can cover a lot of your everyday needs.It has traceable pipeline building, experiment tracking, data validation, dataset versioning and other best practices of enterprise MLOps pipelines adapted for the small scale.Large companies usually have their needs met with large scale MLOps tools and platforms (mlflow, dvc, sagemaker, you name it), but I feel like no one in the diverse MLOps landscape really cares about individuals and small teams. I had a chance to experience this as an ML engineer at a start-up. This is what motivated me to start building this tool with hopes that other people can avoid doing the same work manually for their own custom experiment tracking or in other aspects.You can learn more about Cascade in the documentation, or contribute on GitHub.Here is what you’ll need (I used Python 3.11.12 for this)pip install 'cascade-ml>=0.15.1,<0.16.0'pip install 'scikit-learn'Let’s create minimal version of an ML model training script. It will have all the main parts of a regular training script.1. Loading and preprocessing data2. Creating and configuring a model3. Training and evaluation4. Results savingfrom sklearn.datasets import load_digitsfrom sklearn.model_selection import train_test_splitfrom sklearn.svm import SVCfrom sklearn.metrics import f1_scorex, y = load_digits(return_X_y=True)x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(x, y)model = SVC(C=1, kernel="linear")model.fit(x_train, y_train)preds = model.predict(x_test)f1 = f1_score(y_test, preds, average="macro")print(f1)with open("digits_svc.pkl", "wb") as f: pickle.dump(model, f)Complexity aside a lot of small-scale ML projects have the same look and structure. Keeping the same overall structure will not allow you much in terms of reproducibility. When the experiment is run you have only binary artifact for a model without knowing which code or parameter values produced it.Enable experiment tracking with CascadeLet’s see how we can update the same script to be more traceable using Cascade.from sklearn.datasets import load_digitsfrom sklearn.model_selection import train_test_splitfrom sklearn.svm import SVCfrom cascade.lines import ModelLinefrom cascade.utils.sklearn import SkModel, SkMetricx, y = load_digits(return_X_y=True)x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(x, y)params = dict(C=1, kernel="linear")model = SkModel(blocks=[SVC(**params)], **params)model.fit(x_train, y_train)model.evaluate(x_train, y_train, metrics=[SkMetric("f1_score", average="macro")])line = ModelLine("digits")line.save(model)The main thing about Cascade’s design is that not much have been added. However, a lot have changed.With only this small modification you already get — every time you will run this script, you will automatically save the model checkpoint along with hyperparameters and metric values!Let’s break it down step by step.Here we wrap our scikit-learn model into Cascade model that integrates with sklearn and will automatically handle a lot of tracking for us. Note that we pass parameters to the block itself and also explicitly pass them to the wrapper. Wrapper will track model configuration as model’s params. Method for training will work the same way as before wrapping.model = SkModel(blocks=[SVC(**params)], **params)model.fit(x_train, y_train)Next is the evaluation stage. Here we provide the data for inference, ground truth labels and a list of metrics. Metrics are treated specially in Cascade. You can learn more in my other post below.model.evaluate(x_test, y_test, metrics=[SkMetric("f1_score", average="macro")])Finally instead of pickling the model manually we use a special structure called Line. It will manage a series of sequential models for us. Each time you call line.save() it will create a new directory and store model checkpoint and everything that is needed there for you.line = ModelLine("digits")line.save(model)Everything is handled automatically and stored locally — no clouds, tokens or servers are needed. You can access the folder that was created and see the contents of it if you want..├── 00000│ │ └── pipeline.pkl│ ├── model.pkl└── meta.jsonLet’s see how meta.json file at the model folder looks.[ { "name": "cascade.utils.sklearn.sk_model.SkModel", "description": null, "comments": [], "type": "model", "created_at": "2025-05-05T18:11:39.647946+00:00", "metrics": [ "name": "f1_score", "value": 0.9817632547714215, "created_at": "2025-05-05T18:11:39.706641+00:00" ], "C": 1, }, "pipeline": "Pipeline(steps=[('0', SVC(C=1, kernel='linear'))])", "path": "/home/user/digits/00000", "slug": "sepia_kingfisher_of_success", "saved_at": "2025-05-05T18:11:39.756233+00:00", "python_version": "3.11.12 (main, Apr 9 2025, 08:55:54) [GCC 11.4.0]", "user": "user", }Everything we need to know about the experiment is stored in this file. We can keep it close to the binary pipeline.pkl to know how it was produced.For example here we enabled reproducibility by tracking all the relevant parameters. "params": { "C": 1, "degree": 3We can pull them up whenever to reproduce the results. Notice that we also tracked the time and environment information without adding much to the code, Cascade does this automatically. It will also detect if you are inside a git repository and remember the current commit. "saved_at": "2025-05-05T18:11:39.756233+00:00", "python_version": "3.11.12 (main, Apr 9 2025, 08:55:54) [GCC 11.4.0]", "host": "host"Analyze results of multiple experimentsLet’s update the parameters in the script and run the same thing a couple more times. Here I changed parameter C with linear kernel and in another series of experiments changed the kernel to rbf. Everything else in the script remained unchanged.params = dict(C=1, kernel="linear")params = dict(C=3, kernel="linear")params = dict(C=2, kernel="linear")params = dict(C=1, kernel="rbf")params = dict(C=2, kernel="rbf")params = dict(C=3, kernel="rbf")You can always manually go through meta.json files for small number of experiments, but as the number goes up you need tools to access meta. And Cascade has a number of tools to help you make sense of a series of experiments.If you like pandas there is MetricViewer for you. You can do the following and get a nice pd.DataFrame of your parameters and metrics given the Line object.from cascade.meta import MetricViewermv.tableline num created_at saved C \0 digits 0 2025-05-10 19:19:33.012345+00:00 a few seconds after 1 1 digits 1 2025-05-10 19:19:52.331062+00:00 a few seconds after 2 2 digits 2 2025-05-10 19:19:54.168451+00:00 a few seconds after 3 3 digits 3 2025-05-10 19:20:02.490522+00:00 a few seconds after 1 4 digits 4 2025-05-10 19:20:04.419784+00:00 a few seconds after 2 5 digits 5 2025-05-10 19:20:06.181122+00:00 a few seconds after 3 kernel tags comment_count link_count name value 0 linear [] 0 0 f1_score 0.976085 1 linear [] 0 0 f1_score 0.984136 2 linear [] 0 0 f1_score 0.981162 3 rbf [] 0 0 f1_score 0.984601 4 rbf [] 0 0 f1_score 0.983684 5 rbf [] 0 0 f1_score 0.984712Using this table you can aggregate and filter results for model selection. Based on the values of your metrics you can see what is working or what is not in terms of parameter values.If you are a fan of working inside the console you can try out Cascade CLI. It has very simple query language for your results. Locate inside the line folder (in our case ‘digits’) and run:cascade query \params.C params.kernel 'metrics[0].value' \sort 'metrics[0].value' desc────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────params.C params.kernel metrics[0].value ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────3 rbf 0.984711961966202 1 rbf 0.9846013189866026 2 linear 0.9841358519257071 2 rbf 0.9836841084071789 3 linear 0.9811622508398392 1 linear 0.976085139779728 ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────Finished: 2025-05-10 22:40:46.478301+03:00Returned rows: 6Now we got the results ordered by the value of the first metric, which is the only metrics we saved.Frequently when running machine learning experiments there is a need to label experiments for quick reference and express thoughts that should be attached to some of the instances.Here is how you can do that with Cascade. Navigate to any model from our digits line.cd digits/00000cascade tag add baselineYou can verify using query inside the line:cd ..cascade query slug tags filter '"baseline"' in tags────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────slug tags ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────adaptable_tapir_of_adventure ['baseline'] ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────Finished: 2025-05-10 22:45:41.958603+03:00Time: 0.0016sOr by using tag display command:You can also comment on any experiment. It is proven to be a very useful tool in practice. You are not only reflecting on the experiment results, but also store your thoughts with the results themselves. This relation is what makes things clearer and more reproducible.To add a comment to a particular model (you can also comment on lines, why not) you need to locate to the folder and run:After that you will be prompted to type anything.You can view comments usingChanging values in your script, saving it and running again can be a little tedious. To ease things up Cascade has a system of configs.Let’s update the previous script to have a config instead of plain dictionary of parameters.from cascade.base import Configfrom cascade.lines import ModelLinefrom cascade.utils.sklearn import SkMetric, SkModelfrom sklearn.datasets import load_digitsfrom sklearn.model_selection import train_test_splitfrom sklearn.svm import SVC C = 3x, y = load_digits(return_X_y=True)x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(x, y)model = SkModel(blocks=[SVC(**CFG().to_dict())], **CFG().to_dict())model.fit(x_train, y_train)# This will track things automaticallymodel.add_config()model.evaluate(x_test, y_test, metrics=[SkMetric("f1_score", average="macro")])# This is for non-empty logprint(CFG().to_dict())line = ModelLine("digits")line.save(model)Now we are ready to see the magic of cascade run command. Navigate into the folder with the script and run:cascade run script.py -logYou should see similar output:You are about to run intro_post.pyThe config is:{'C': 3, 'kernel': 'rbf'}The arguments you passed:Confirm? [y/N]:Since we didn’t change the parameters the dict is empty — the script will run with default ones.Let’s see what changed — the model folder is now has files folder filled with:1. The cascade_config.json file with all our parameters2. The cascade_overrides.json file with our empty dict3. The cascade_run.log — the log fileTo change the default values you should mention them in the call like this:cascade run script.py --log -y --C 2 --kernel '"linear"'Notice the -y option which will allow us to run without confirmation.You are about to run intro_post.pyThe config is:{'C': 3, 'kernel': 'rbf'}The arguments you passed:{'C': 2, 'kernel': 'linear'}The prompt will show us the original log and the changes we’ve made. The magic of the command is that the source code remains unchanged — the changes in config are applied on-the-fly and never touch the file, which allows for concurrent runs with different configurations for example.You can dive deeper into documentation and start from tutorial.

item.title: A Guide to OpenTelemetry Collector
item.author: Vansh Uppal
item.content:encoded: It’s architecture and componentsOpenTelemetry is an open source observability framework that provides protocol and tools for collecting and exporting telemetry data, i.e. metrics, traces and logs. OpenTelemetry provides a consistent format for instrumenting, generating, gathering, and exporting application telemetry data — namely metrics, logs, and traces — to monitoring platforms for analysis.The three pillars of observability: Logs, metrics and tracesObservability is built on three core pillars: , , and . are numeric representations of system behavior over time, such as CPU usage, memory consumption, or request rates. They are ideal for trend analysis, alerting, and capacity planning. The main benefit of metrics is that they provide real-time insight into the state of resources. If you want to know how responsive your application is or identify anomalies that could be early signs of a performance issue, metrics are a key source of visibility.are records of events, warnings, and errors that occur within a software environment. Well-structured logs provide valuable context — such as which request was made, by which user ID, and to which endpoint — making it easier to understand and debug application behaviour.provide a detailed view of a request’s journey through various components in a distributed system, helping to pinpoint latency, errors, and bottlenecks. They record how long each component takes to process the request and pass it to the next, offering precise performance insights.This holistic approach is essential for diagnosing issues, optimising performance, and building resilient, reliable applicationsNow that we have a basic understanding of what OpenTelemetry is and the types of data required for effective application monitoring, let’s dive deeper into the OpenTelemetry Collector and explore how it works.What is OpenTelemetry Collector?The is a critical component in the OpenTelemetry ecosystem that receives, processes, and exports telemetry data — logs, metrics, and traces — from instrumented applications and systems. Its primary purpose is to standardise and centralise telemetry data handling, making it easier to route data to one or more observability backends like Prometheus, Jaeger, Tempo, or third-party services such as Datadog or New Relic.One of the major benefits of using the Collector is that it offloads the logic and complexity of exporting telemetry data from the application itself. This reduces the processing overhead on your services, and more importantly, decouples them from specific telemetry exporters or backends. If you need to switch or modify a backend, you can simply update the Collector’s configuration without making changes across multiple applications. This makes your architecture more modular, maintainable, and adaptable.By abstracting away monitoring-related concerns, the Collector ensures that your core business services remain focused on delivering business value, not managing observability plumbing. In other words, the application architecture stays clean and independent from changes in your monitoring stack.These services can include your applications integrated with OpenTelemetry SDKs, databases, and managed services.Deployment strategy otel-collector1. Horizontal scaling the otel collector.2. Sidecar or DaemonSet Agents with a Central GatewayThis approach can be effectively adopted in Kubernetes. You can deploy the OpenTelemetry Collector as a or a . In the sidecar model, a collector runs alongside each application pod and handles telemetry for that specific app. In the DaemonSet model, a single collector agent runs on each node and collects telemetry from multiple applications running on that node. These agents then forward the data to a centralized OpenTelemetry Collector.A DaemonSet ensures that a copy of a specific pod runs on in the Kubernetes cluster. This is useful for running background system services like log shippers or metric collectors.The centralized OpenTelemetry Collector typically has a , but the proxy agents (sidecars or DaemonSets) can have tailored to the needs of each service or node.Data collection, processing, and exporting in the OpenTelemetry Collector are orchestrated through . You can configure the Collector to run , each tailored for a specific signal type — , , or .Each pipeline consists of the following stages:: Components that from various sources (e.g.Databases, services. gateways). (optional): Intermediate stages that transform, batch, filter, or enrich the data before export.: Components that to external systems like observability platforms or storage backends.Pipelines are flexible and reusable:A single can feed data into .The same can be shared across but each pipeline will have it’s own instance of exporter.This modular design allows you to build efficient, scalable observability pipelines without duplicating configuration or logic.A pipeline defines the path that data follows in the OpenTelemetry Collector — from receiving the data, to processing or modifying it, and finally exporting it. Pipelines can operate on three types of telemetry data: traces, metrics, and logs. The type of data a pipeline handles is specified in its configuration.Each component within the pipeline (receivers, processors, and exporters) must support the data type defined for that pipeline. If any component does not support the required data type, the Collector will report a pipeline.ErrSignalNotSupported error when loading the configuration.The following diagram typically represents how a pipeline is structured:Pipelines can have one or more receivers. Data from all receivers is sent to the first processor, which processes the data and then passes it to the next processor in the sequence. A processor may also drop data if it is performing sampling or filtering. This continues until the last processor pushes the processed data to the exporters.Each exporter receives a copy of the data. The last processor uses a fan-out mechanism (fanoutconsumer) to distribute the data to multiple exporters.The pipeline is constructed during the Collector’s startup, based on the pipeline definitions provided in the configuration file.A typical pipeline configuration includes a list of receivers, processors, and exporters that define how telemetry data is received, transformed, and sent out.service: pipelines: # section that can contain multiple subsections, one per pipeline traces: # type of the pipeline receivers: [otlp, zipkin] processors: [memory_limiter, batch] exporters: [otlp, zipkin]Receivers are the entry points for telemetry data into the OpenTelemetry Collector. They accept data in various formats and protocols such as OTLP, Jaeger, Prometheus, Zipkin, StatsD, and more. Essentially, receivers are the first component in a pipeline — they collect incoming telemetry data and pass it along for processing and exporting.receivers: otlp: grpc: pipelines: traces: # a pipeline of “traces” type receivers: [otlp] processors: [memory_limiter, batch] exporters: [otlp] traces/2: # another pipeline of “traces” type receivers: [otlp] exporters: [otlp]In this example, we define a single receiver named otlp, which listens for gRPC traffic on port 4317. This receiver is configured to accept telemetry data (in OTLP format) over gRPC.We then define two separate pipelines of the traces type. Both pipelines use the same otlp receiver, meaning that the incoming telemetry data on port 4317 is sent into both pipelines. Each pipeline can then apply its own set of processors and exporters independently.This setup allows for flexible processing paths while keeping the receiver configuration centralized.A pipeline can contain processors connected in a sequence. The first processor receives data from one or more receivers configured in the pipeline, and the last processor sends the data to one or more exporters. The processors in between receive data only from the previous processor and forward it to the next one in the chain.Processors are used to modify or transform the data — for example, by adding or removing attributes from spans. They can also filter out data (like the probabilistic_sampler processor) or even generate new telemetry data.The same processor name can be used in the processors section of multiple pipelines. In such cases, the same configuration is applied, but each pipeline gets its own separate instance of the processor. This means that while the configuration is shared, the internal state is not — each instance maintains its own state. For example, if a batch processor is used in multiple pipelines, each pipeline has its own dedicated batch processor instance, all configured in the same way.processors: batch: timeout: 10s pipelines: traces: # a pipeline of “traces” type receivers: [zipkin] exporters: [otlp] traces/2: # another pipeline of “traces” type receivers: [otlp] exporters: [otlp]The timeout refers to the maximum amount of time the batch processor will wait before sending a batch of telemetry data, even if the batch hasn’t reached the send_batch_size.: The processor will try to collect up to 10,000 items (spans, metrics, or logs) before sending them to the next component (exporter).: If it hasn’t collected 10,000 items within 10 seconds, it will send whatever it has anyway.This ensures that data doesn’t get stuck waiting too long if traffic is low. It helps reduce latency in exporting and keeps a steady flow of data through the pipeline. are responsible for forwarding telemetry data to external destinations — typically over a network. However, not all exporters send data to remote systems. For instance, the debug exporter writes telemetry data to the local logs, which is useful for troubleshooting.The OpenTelemetry Collector allows you to define multiple exporters of the same type, even within the same pipeline. For example, you can configure two otlp exporters that each send data to a different OTLP endpoint.exporters: otlp: grpc: endpoint: localhost:14250 pipelines: traces: # a pipeline of “traces” type receivers: [zipkin] processors: [memory_limiter] exporters: [otlp] traces/2: # another pipeline of “traces” type receivers: [otlp] exporters: [otlp]The OpenTelemetry Collector offers a powerful, flexible, and vendor-neutral way to receive, process, and export telemetry data — whether it’s traces, metrics, or logs. By decoupling observability logic from your core application, it helps reduce complexity, improve scalability, and make monitoring infrastructure more adaptable to change.Choosing between an and pattern depends on your architecture and scale, will be happy to set on a call to discuss what can be used to fit in your case, as one is not better than other, it totally depends on your use case.As the landscape of observability matures, the OpenTelemetry Collector will continue to be at the core of a reliable, scalable, and future-proof telemetry pipeline.
